The simulation results show that the device can work over a wide range of frequencies, from 2.67 GHz to 6.08 GHz, with S11-10 dB. It implies that it can work on essential Sub-6 GHz communication frequencies. The antenna's highest gain is 8.25 dB, and it keeps a stable broadside radiation pattern throughout its entire range of operation. Due to its compact size, wide bandwidth, and high radiation efficiency, the proposed design is well suited for 5 G, WLAN, and WiMAX wireless communication systems. In this work, a compact square patch antenna integrating asymmetric corner modification and a 45° slanted slot is proposed. Unlike conventional corner-truncated or slot-loaded designs, the combined perturbation simultaneously enhances impedance bandwidth and surface current distribution, enabling continuous broadband operation from 2.67 to 6.08 GHz on a low-cost FR4 substrate.

title: A Hybrid Quantum-Post-Quantum Framework for Secure Data Encryption Using Adaptive Steganography
authors: Rajkumar Kuppuswami, Rajanikanth Sanku, Suresh. A, Pradeep Chintale, Kiruthiga S
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Integrated Intelligence and Communication Systems (ICIICS)
abstract: In recent times, secure data transmission become crucial requirement because of increasing growth of threats and emergence of quantum computing technologies. However, existing Quantum Key Distribution-Advanced Encryption Standard-Least Significant Bit (QKD-AES-LSB) struggled with two main limitations such as restricted resistance to future quantum attacks because of dependence on classical encryption and vulnerability of static LSB steganography for improved Machine Learning (ML) approaches. To address these challenges, Quantum Key Distribution-Post-Quantum Cryptography-Adaptive Steganographic Encryption framework (QKD-PQC-ASE) is proposed. Primarily, secret data and cover images are gathered then quantum key is generated using E91 protocol. Next, post-quantum cryptographic key encapsulation is applied to strengthened the generated kay and ensure long-term quantum resistance. Further, secret data is encrypted employing quantum-resilient encryption mechanism and combined using cryptographic hashing. Furthermore, encrypted data is embedded in to cover image through quantum-randomness-guided adaptive steganography for resisting improved steganalysis attacks. Finally, proposed QKD-PQC-ASE framework obtains improved resistance against future quantum attacks and enhanced steganalysis by maintaining low computational overhead. Therefore, proposed QKD-PQC-ASE framework achieved greater results in terms of NPCR of 99.84 % and a UACI of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 7. 9 2 \%}$</tex> which demonstrates efficiency of framework for secure and future-ready data encryption in quantum-aware communication environments.

title: Freezetune ASR: A Selective Freezing and Genetic Algorithm (GA) Based Selective Fine Tuning for Accent Robust English Speech Recognition
authors: Dingyi Liu
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Integrated Intelligence and Communication Systems (ICIICS)
abstract: Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) has been successful in controlled settings, particularly when trained in clean English corpora like LibriSpeech. But when systems have diverse accents, dialects, or spontaneous speech conditions, then recognition accuracy decreases to a very low value. Previous research demonstrated that spectral features with Convolutional Neural NetworkBi Long Short Term Memory (CNN BiLSTM) constructions are effective at accent recognition albeit with large data sets and extensive retraining. On the same note, traditional transfer learning methods tend to experience doubt in determining whether to freeze or fine tune the layers and hence inefficiency and biasness when used on accent rich or noisy speech. These issues point to the necessity of resource efficient approaches that would compromise the generalization and adaptive strategies. In this study, the issue being dealt with is that of worsening the performance of ASR in non-native English speakers and in environments that are rich in accent. The proposed model, namely FreezeTune ASR, is more fair and robust, as freezes the low level CNN layers trained on the LibriSpeech and performs Genetic Algorithm (GA) based selective fine tuning to upper CNN and BiLSTM layers using the Common Voice. This evolutionary optimization provides a systematic selection of trainable layers and eliminates the randomness of manual selection. Experimental justification proves the fact that this selective adaptation enhances accuracy and lowers the occurrence of errors as compared to full retraining and standard transfer learning and supports the fact that GA guided freezing and fine tuning are effective methods of accent robust speech recognition.

title: Visual Orientation Posterior for Post-Retrieval UAV Geo-Localization
authors: Chaoyue Lv, Yuying Du, Desheng Chen, Yunlong Xie, Afiq Herdika Sulistya, Mengqi Ji
abstract: Cross-view UAV geo-localization is often framed as image retrieval, but correct top-1 retrieval does not guarantee accurate final localization. We propose a lightweight post-retrieval verification framework based on a visual orientation posterior (VOP), which predicts a discrete posterior over candidate orientations and allocates sparse verification to a few geometry-promising hypotheses. This lightweight design is practically important for onboard deployment on small and medium UAVs, where edge processors impose strict memory and latency budgets and make dense verification difficult to accommodate. Trained with supervision derived from final localization quality, our method consistently outperforms sparse and rotation-enumeration baselines as well as LoFTR on final localization metrics under our protocol, and narrows the gap to dense DKM while remaining much more efficient. Experiments on the GTA-UAV and UAV-VisLoc datasets validate the effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: Spatiotemporal Modelling of Urban Crime: Chicago and Los Angeles
authors: Shoaib, Chittesh K, Ramkumar S, Gobinath R, Stephen R, Rajasekaran N
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Integrated Intelligence and Communication Systems (ICIICS)
abstract: Urban crime exhibits complex spatiotemporal patterns requiring advanced analytical frameworks. This study develops a comparative crime modeling system for Chicago and Los Angeles using official datasets (2010-2025) comprising over 7 million incidents. Data were processed using H3 hierarchical spatial indexing and enriched with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$20+$</tex> engineered features. We evaluated statistical baselines (ARIMA, Poisson regression) against deep learning models (NBEATS, NHiTS, ConvLSTM) and applied Kernel Density Estimation for hotspot detection. NHiTS achieved superior performance with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}^{2}=0.455$</tex>, MAE <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=38.44$</tex>, and SMAPE <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=6.45 \%$</tex> for Los Angeles, outperforming ARIMA (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}^{2}=-0.089$</tex>) by 600%. Chicago results showed <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}^{2}= 0.391$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{MAE}=42.48$</tex> for NHiTS versus <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}^{\mathbf{2}}=-\mathbf{5. 5 0}$</tex> for ARIMA. Spatial analysis revealed that less than 1% of hexagonal areas (74 in Chicago, 153 in Los Angeles) contained over 50% of total crime incidents. Temporal patterns differed: Chicago showed pronounced seasonal cycles, while Los Angeles exhibited stronger diurnal rhythms. Theoretical policy simulations suggest targeted interventions could reduce incidents by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 7. 2 - 4 2. 6 \%}$</tex> under optimal conditions. This multi-model framework advances urban crime forecasting methodology while emphasizing ethical safeguards and community oversight.

title: Centroid Stability Index-Driven Mahalanobis Clustering for Robust Mental Health Assessment of College Students
authors: Yuling Long
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Integrated Intelligence and Communication Systems (ICIICS)
abstract: In recent years, accurately addressing student mental health issues is extremely important. Once accurately measured, students can often receive early intervention to prevent further decline. Centroid instability (regarding traditional clustering methods) and sensitivity to initialization often creates unreliable cluster classifications during psychological evaluations. Therefore, a mental health assessment model is proposed that consists of K-means clustering and utilization of Mahalanobis distances along with a Centroid Stability Index (CSI) as a newly proposed refinement mechanism. In the initial step, data noise is removed using an interquartile range (IQR) approach; next, data is normalized using a min-max technique. K-means clustering is applied using the boundaries established from the initial two steps, and a CSI method measures the stability at which the centroid converges. Centroids that do not converge reliably are then re-initialized before attempting to Cluster then measure using Mahalanobis distances to determine correlation between multiple psychological variables. The experimental evaluation produces more stable cluster results and increases accuracy compared to traditional methods. Finally, the CSI-based approach provides a reliable statistical framework to use for assessing student mental health in academic institutions. The proposed KMC-CSI outperforms the existing model such as L-LR in terms of accuracy (0.9765), specificity (0.9732) and recall (0.9865).

title: A Data Mining Driven Machine Learning Framework: Intelligent Early Detection of Kidney and Liver Diseases Using Electronic Health Records
authors: Marowa Jahan, Md. Ashfaq Ayub, Syed Mostafa Tahsinul Islam, Ijajul Haque Vuban, Sharfuddin Mahmood
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence &amp; Networking (QPAIN)
abstract: Early diagnosis of chronic diseases is important in enhancing patient outcomes and lowering the cost of health care. Two of the most common chronic diseases in the world include chronic kidney disease and liver disease which tend to develop without being detected until they reach serious stages. In this paper, the authors give a data mining-based machine learning model with the early prediction of chronic kidney and liver diseases based on electronic health record data. The statistical methods proposed to be used in the framework are correlation analysis and chi-square feature selection alongside the machine learning classifiers, which are gini index random forest and decision tree using gradient boosting. There are two publicly available datasets of healthcare data (two in total) which are used to check the generalizability of the framework to the diseases with dissimilar clinical features and data scales. The experiments demonstrate that the models of ensemble learning are much better than the baseline classifiers in their recall accuracy and overall predictive performance. The most influential clinical features that can predict the disease are also brought to the fore in the study, hence enhancing model interpretability and clinical relevance. The proposed framework can be used to support health workers in the early diagnosis and decision making and it shows the usefulness of the statistical feature analysis combined with the ensemble machine learning techniques in chronic disease prediction.

title: Optimized Random Number Generator for Power and Area Efficiency
authors: S Chandana, Abhinav Movva, Rachana P, Rachana H D, Veena M B
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Integrated Intelligence and Communication Systems (ICIICS)
abstract: Hardware-based Random Number Generators (RNGs) are fundamental to area-constrained applications such as stochastic computing and Monte Carlo-based accelerators. However, conventional architectures, specifically those that use Cellular Automata Shift Registers (CASRs), often impose significant routing complexity and high switching activity, which drives up power consumption and silicon area in ASIC implementations. To address these inefficiencies, this research introduces a streamlined RNG architecture that replaces resource-heavy CASRs with a hybrid integration of the Xoroshiro algorithm, a Linear Feedback Shift Register (LFSR), and a jitter-based ring oscillator. This configuration prioritizes logic-efficient rotations and minimal state transitions to reduce the hardware footprint. Following physical implementation, the design was validated through the NIST Statistical Test Suite. Experimental results demonstrate a notable reduction in both power dissipation and silicon area compared to traditional RNG+LFSR+CASR benchmarks. Ultimately, this optimized hybrid model successfully balances randomness quality with physical efficiency, offering a scalable entropy source for high-performance, power-sensitive hardware accelerators.

title: Accurate Approximation of Higher-Order Statistics of Channel Capacity in Log-Normal Fading
authors: Yassine Mouchtak, Faissal El Bouanani, Meiling Li
abstract: This paper investigates the higher-order statistics (HOS) of channel capacity (CC) in log-normal (LN) fading channels. Unlike prior works that rely on infinite series or highly sensitive approximations, we propose a simple closed-form approximation of the HOS of CC and its asymptotic expression in the high signal-to-noise ratio regime, based on which the amount of dispersion and the corresponding reliability of the signal throughput have been evaluated. The accuracy of the proposed expressions is verified through numerical simulations, showing excellent agreement with exact results across a wide range of channel parameters. These findings offer practical insights for the design of reliable communication systems over LN fading channels, while significantly reducing computational complexity.

title: DysLSTM: A Deep Sequential Framework for Dyslexia Prediction with Comparative Analysis of Baseline and Advanced Models
authors: Mahmudul Haque Shakir, Md. Saef Ullah Miah, Omar Faruk, Rifat Tasnim Anannya
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence &amp; Networking (QPAIN)
abstract: Dyslexia is one of the most common types of learning disorders, affecting both the reading and decoding of words in children and adults. The early detection is of paramount importance for timely intervention and yet the widely-used detection approaches in traditional systems are expensive, time-consuming and subjective. In this work, we propose DysLSTM: a robust deep learning framework that relies on Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks to accurately predict dyslexia. We trained and tested it on an open dataset, collecting 3,644 individuals, with elaborate data pre-processing, feature mapping, scaling and balancing. Experimental results on multiple baseline machine learning (Logistic Regression, Random Forest, SVM) and deep learning (CNN, GRU, RNN) models are shown that DysLSTM outperforms all of them with 97% accuracy and high precision-recall scores as well as a Cohen's Kappa score of 0.91, which suggests that DysLSTM has nearly perfect agreement.

title: Sensorless Control of Dual Three-Phase PMSM Using a Subspace Decoupling-Based Fuzzy Super-Twisting Sliding Mode Observer
authors: Luhui Xu, Zhiguo Zhao, Peng Tang
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: To address the chattering, parameter sensitivity, and limited operating-condition adaptability of conventional sliding mode observers (SMOs) in sensorless control of dual three-phase permanent magnet synchronous motors (DTP-PMSMs), this paper proposes a subspace-decoupling-based integrated method combining a fuzzy super-twisting sliding mode observer (F-STSMO) and grey wolf optimization (GWO). In the proposed method, fuzzy control is integrated with the super-twisting sliding mode observer (STSMO) to construct a fuzzy time-varying-gain observer. The stability of the observer under time-varying disturbances is demonstrated, which effectively suppresses chattering and improves the observation performance over a wide dynamic range. By exploiting the multiple degrees of freedom of DTP-PMSMs, a GWO-based parameter estimation algorithm is developed. The sensitivity to initial settings and the convergence conditions are discussed, enabling high-accuracy real-time motor parameter estimation and enhancing the robustness of the STSMO against parameter variations. Furthermore, the Lyapunov stability constraints of the integrated F-STSMO and GWO method are analyzed. Experimental results show that the proposed integrated F-STSMO and GWO sensorless control method achieves a resistance estimation error of 0.9% for the DTP-PMSM, reduces the rotor position error caused by parameter variations by 88.6%, and keeps the rotor position error fluctuation within 2.51° under both steady-state and dynamic operating conditions.

title: LLM-Guided Adaptive Compensator: Bringing Adaptivity to Robotic Feedback Control With Large Language Model_supp2-3700674.mp4
authors: Yuxi Lu
abstract: Recent advances in code generation and reasoning have enabled the growing use of large language models (LLMs) in robotics. The design of traditional robotic feedback controllers often requires extensive expert knowledge and iterative computational effort, motivating the exploration of LLM-assisted controller development. However, existing efforts are largely restricted to overly simplified systems, provide limited comparison with human-designed controllers, and lack validation on real-world robotic platforms. To address these gaps, this work investigates the use of LLMs in robotic feedback control by focusing on adaptive control and proposing an LLM-guided adaptive compensator. Instead of generating a complete controller from scratch, the LLM is guided to design a compensator that modulates the response of an unknown system to match that of a predefined reference system, thereby achieving adaptivity. The proposed approach is evaluated using five adaptive controllers on a two-degree-of-freedom (DoF) soft robot and a one-DoF shoulder joint of a humanoid robot. Both simulation and real-world experiments demonstrate that the LLM-guided adaptive compensator achieves performance comparable to four conventional adaptive controllers. Lyapunov-based analysis further establishes the stability and generalization capability of the proposed compensator, while analysis of the reasoning process suggests a more structured design approach. This study introduces a novel framework for integrating LLMs into robotic feedback control, with promising practical applicability. Note to Practitioners—This paper proposes a framework that uses large language models (LLMs) to design an adaptive compensator for robotic feedback control. Existing LLM-based approaches have largely been limited to simple systems and to adjusting controller gains, which restricts their reliability and applicability in real automation settings. In contrast, the proposed framework enables an LLM to design a compensator that modulates the response of an existing controller to track a predefined reference system, thereby achieving adaptivity. We provide theoretical analysis, drawing on Lyapunov stability theory and analysis of the LLM reasoning process, to demonstrate the stability and generalization capability of the compensator. We also compare its performance with that of several human-designed adaptive controllers, showing comparable control performance along with improved usability and design efficiency. These results support validation on real-world robots, where both simulations and prototype experiments confirm improved performance. From a practical standpoint, the proposed approach is zero-shot, requires only the desired system response as input, and uses open prompts that generalize across different robot platforms. This eliminates the need for a complete system model. Although demonstrated on robotic feedback control, the framework is applicable to other feedback control domains. A current limitation is that the upper bound of system complexity that can be reliably handled remains to be established, motivating future benchmarking efforts.

title: A Control-Oriented High-Power-Factor Interleaved PFC-PSFB EV Charger for Intelligent Two-Wheeler Charging Applications
authors: Anusree Shibindas, Singaravelan Arumugam, N. Samanvita
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Integrated Intelligence and Communication Systems (ICIICS)
abstract: The increasing demand for compact and grid-compliant chargers for two-wheeler electric vehicles (2WEVs) necessitates advanced control strategies that simultaneously ensure input power quality and safe battery charging. This paper presents a control-architecture-oriented simulation study of a two-stage EV charger employing a conventional interleaved boost power factor correction (IBPFC) converter followed by a phase-shifted full-bridge (PSFB) DC-DC converter. On the grid side, digitally enhanced average current-mode control with adaptive current reference shaping and zero-cross softening is applied to improve the power factor and reduce low-order harmonics. On the battery side, a supervisory constant-current/constant-voltage (CC-CV) charging control with hysteresis-based mode management and smooth transition logic is implemented. MATLAB/Simulink-based evaluation demonstrates near-unity power factor, low input current THD, stable DC-link voltage regulation, and smooth CC-CV charging transitions. The results confirm that the proposed coordinated and adaptive control architecture enables intelligent and reliable operation of two-stage EV chargers.

title: Ai-Powered Simulation for Smart City Traffic Flow Management
authors: Chirag M. Shetty, Lavanya. G, Mahammad Altaf, Nagaraja. S R, Robin Rohit Vincent, Prashanth Kannadaguli
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Integrated Intelligence and Communication Systems (ICIICS)
abstract: The Digital Twin technology is becoming one of the central elements of a smart city development since traffic conditions may be recreated, studied and optimized within a safe virtual environment. It is a report on an AI-based Digital Twin system which real-time perception (traffic), intelligent reasoning and simulation-based validation are integrated in a single loop. The local training of a YOLOv8 model assigned a pre-labeled dataset downloaded via Roboflow was able to allow vehicle detection and tracking using a web video feed. Google Gemini interprets the traffic data and implies practical changes, which include signal optimization, rearrangement of lanes, and congestion-reduction techniques. These recommendations are then automatically converted to structured commands and built in and immediately run in an interactive simulation module where baseline circumstances and improved circumstances can be compared side by side. The findings reveal the presence of smoother flow, less welter, and more informed decision making, which proves the fact that the intersection of computer vision and generative AI and Digital Twin simulation can lead to a reliable and adaptive solution to the issue of modern city traffic management.

title: Near-Infrared Spectroscopy with Chemometrics for Non-Destructive Classification of Mineral, Purified, and Tap Water
authors: Omnia Hamdy, Amna H. Faid, Tawfik Ismail
abstract: This paper presents a rapid and non-destructive approach to distinguishing mineral, purified, and tap water using near-infrared (NIR) transmission spectroscopy and chemometrics in the 900-1700 nm spectral region. Following z-score standardisation, Principal Component Analysis (PCA) was implemented to investigate spectral variance. The first principal component accounted for 67.78% of the overall variance, showing a significant discriminative structure in the dataset. Fisher discriminant analysis identified crucial wavelengths at 1155 nm, 1159 nm, and 1244 nm as the most informative spectral characteristics corresponding to second-overtone C-H and combination-band O-H absorption. Stratified 10-fold cross-validation was used to evaluate three supervised classification algorithms: k-Nearest Neighbour (KNN), Support Vector Machine with radial basis function kernel (SVM-RBF), and Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA). With only one misclassification out of 120 samples, the confusion matrix revealed that the SVM-RBF and LDA classifiers had a typical classification accuracy of 99.2%. These results reveal that NIR spectroscopy offers an immediate, reagent-free, and highly sensitive technique for water source verification and quality monitoring when combined with proper pre-processing and chemometrics.

title: A Unified Full Speed Domain Rotor Position and Speed Estimation Method Based on Adaptive Multifrequency State Observer for IPMSM Sensorless Drives
authors: Yanping Zhang, Zhonggang Yin, Fengtao Gao, Jing Liu
abstract: A unified full speed domain rotor position and speed estimation method based on an adaptive multifrequency state observer (AMFSO) for interior permanent magnet synchronous motors (IPMSMs) sensorless drives is proposed in this article. First, the AMFSO is used to estimate the fundamental frequency back electromotive force (BEMF) and high frequency (HF) BEMF. The fundamental frequency BEMF estimation loop of AMFSO has bandpass characteristics without phase shift at the motor operating frequency, and has notch filter characteristics at the frequency of injecting HF voltage. The HF BEMF estimation loop of AMFSO has bandpass characteristics without amplitude attenuation or phase shift at the frequency of injecting HF voltage, and has notch filter characteristics at the motor operating frequency. Therefore, AMFSO can accurately estimate the fundamental frequency BEMF and HF BEMF. Then, the full speed domain unified virtual BEMF model is established, and the rotor position and speed in the full speed domain are estimated by the unified virtual BEMF model. There is no need to switch the two estimation methods, and the established unified virtual BEMF model has no phase jump when HF voltage is injected or not. Finally, the effectiveness of the proposed method was validated on an IPMSM driver platform.

title: Machine Learning - Driven Student Ranking for Efficient University Accommodation Allocation
authors: Sheikh Khalid Azad, Md Muztahid Hassan, Mohammad Shahidur Rahman, Abdullah Al Noman
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence &amp; Networking (QPAIN)
abstract: The allocation of limited university accommodation in Bangladesh is currently a manual, labor-intensive process prone to human error and subjective bias. While administrative decisions rely on both quantitative metrics (e.g., CGPA, income) and qualitative appeals (e.g., student “Family Situation” narratives), existing automated systems rarely process both simultaneously. To address this, we present a novel, manually annotated dataset comprising 3,454 real-world applicant profiles. We introduce a multimodal machine learning framework that utilizes this dataset's seven distinct attributes-fusing quantitative metrics (e.g., CGPA, Income, Disciplinary Record) with unstructured qualitative appeals-to automate student ranking, quantitatively evaluated for algorithmic fairness across socioeconomic subgroups. We implement a data preprocessing pipeline that standardizes multi-lingual application narratives into a unified English format, enabling the extraction of text features via TF-IDF. While TF-IDF was selected for its computational efficiency and high interpretability in an administrative context, it serves as a robust baseline for capturing key indicators of student hardship. These textual features are fused with socio-economic and academic indicators to train a priority-ranking model. A comprehensive evaluation of 28 algorithms reveals that Random Forest Regression achieves the highest fidelity, replicating human expert decisions with 89% accuracy. By converting subjective student narratives into objective, quantifiable data, this system offers a scalable, transparent, and data-driven approach to resource allocation in higher education.

title: Automated Detection of Pelvic Bone Metastases in Radionuclide Bone Scan Images Using a Dual-Stream Cross-Attention Transformer Model
authors: J. A. Motasim, Anonto Chandra Das, Ratna Biswas, Ferdaus Anam Jibon
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence &amp; Networking (QPAIN)
abstract: The detection of pelvic bone metastasis using radionuclide bone scintigraphy is challenging due to complex anatomical structures and intense urinary bladder uptake, which reduces diagnostic accuracy and creates inter-observer variability. This paper presents a Dual-Stream Cross-Attention Vision Transformer (DSCAVT) model that automatically detects pelvic bone metastasis by simultaneously analyzing anterior and posterior views of bone scans. To enhance diagnostic accuracy, the proposed approach incorporates domain-specific preprocessing techniques, including automated bladder masking, morphological top-hat enhancement, sigmoid-based intensity normalization, and multi-channel image construction. Two Vision Transformer encoders extract global features from each view employ cross-attention mechanisms to perform mutual validation between the views. The model was tested on five-fold stratified cross-validation by using pelvic images in the BS-80k database which is publicly available. On average, we achieve accuracy of 80.3%, where ROC-AUC of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{0. 8 3 6}$</tex>, sensitivity of 74.7%, and specificity of 81.1% are recorded from our experimental model. The cross-attention mechanism exchange pattern information, which enhance diagnostic reliability across diverse cancer type. These finding introduce that our proposed dual-view cross-attention transformer enhances pelvic metastasis detection and provides a reliable computer-aided diagnostic tool for nuclear medicine procedures.

title: MediVoiceAI: A Real-Time, Confidence-Aware Voice Assistant for Healthcare Front-Desk Automation Using Large Language Models
authors: Md. Taslim Arif, Abu Salyh Muhammad Mussa, Abdullah Al Mamun, Mominur Rahman Meem, Md. Ashiqussalehin, Md. Ahsan Habib
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence &amp; Networking (QPAIN)
abstract: Voice assistants have become pivotal in enhancing human-computer interaction, offering intuitive, hands-free interfaces for diverse applications. This paper introduces MediVoiceAI, a real-time, confidence-aware and interruption-adaptive AI voice assistant for safe and low-latency healthcare frontdesk appointment scheduling. The system integrates advanced speech processing and large-scale language modeling to deliver seamless patient interactions, doctor management, and appointment booking. Leveraging Deepgram for high-fidelity Speech-to-Text (STT) transcription and Text-to-Speech (TTS) synthesis, alongside a Cerebras Large Language Model (LLM) for contextaware response generation, MediVoiceAI ensures natural and efficient communication. The architecture employs FastAPI and WebSocket for asynchronous, low-latency real-time interaction, supported by a SQLite database for robust data management. This paper provides a detailed overview of the system's components, including STT, TTS, LLM, and database modules, with a focus on their integration for healthcare-specific tasks. Performance is evaluated using metrics such as Word Error Rate (WER) for transcription accuracy, response latency for interaction efficiency, and Mean Opinion Score (MOS) for speech quality. Experimental results demonstrate that MediVoiceAI achieves high transcription accuracy, low response latency and naturalsounding speech, making it an effective solution for automating healthcare appointment scheduling, doctor authentication, and patient support.

title: Martian Mineral Formation Analysis Based on Martian Mineral Corpus and Knowledge Graph_supp1-3702055.pdf
authors: Yanfei Zhong
abstract: Martian minerals are key indicators of the planet’s geological evolution and potential habitability. Their formation mechanisms record critical evidence of early aqueous activity, surface geochemistry, and geological activities. However, analyzing these mechanisms requires integrating heterogeneous data from spectroscopy, geomorphology, and stratigraphy. Existing studies still rely heavily on manual interpretation of heterogeneous data, resulting in inconsistent conclusions and a time-consuming reasoning process. To address this challenge, we build the first unified Martian Mineral Corpus and Martian Mineral Knowledge Graph (MMKG). The corpus integrates global Mars data products and scientific literature, while the MMKG unifies mineral-related concepts with their semantic and spatiotemporal relations, providing comprehensive data and knowledge support for reasoning. On this basis, RBG<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> is proposed as an intelligent question answering (QA) framework designed for Martian mineral analysis. RBG<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> performs knowledge graph path retrieval under dual constraints: geological vectors distilled from the unified corpus guide multi-hop exploration, and geological knowledge-consistency validates candidate chains. This yields graph paths that are both context-aligned and causally plausible. Leveraging these paths as the backbone, RBG<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> integrates geological context from the corpus and the knowledge graph to perform unified reasoning. Experimental results show that RBG<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> outperforms existing methods on Martian mineral question answering. For mineral formation mechanisms, RBG<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> covers over 90% of key points of experts’ analysis on average and produces reasoning that is both interpretable and traceable, achieving the first reliable and intelligent Martian mineral analysis. Code has been made available at: https://github.com/guoluqingfeng10086/MMQA.

title: Multi-Dimensional Perception of Two-Channel Skin-Stretch: Effects of Cue Pattern and Spatial Configuration
authors: Ching Hei Cheng, Jonathan Eden, Denny Oetomo, Ying Tan
abstract: Assistive robots lack natural proprioceptive feedback, often forcing users to rely on vision. With recent advances, haptic interfaces can now accommodate the multidimensional nature of proprioceptive information. Skin-stretch is one promising haptic modality to provide proprioceptive information that resembles natural body mechanisms and conveys both magnitude and direction information, enabling compact multi-channel designs. While prior studies have examined perceptibility and channel interference, the ability of participants to integrate information across multiple channels remains not well understood. This study evaluates how effectively users can combine two skin-stretch channels to interpret two-dimensional cues. 21 able-bodied participants judged, in collocated and non-collocated placement, the linear projection of stimuli under four patterns of skin-stretch variation. Participants could generally perceive and integrate both channels into a coherent understanding of two-dimensional cues, though some non-uniformity in interpretation was observed. Channel proximity showed no clear effect on performance or workload. These findings provide the first systematic investigation of multi-channel integration in skin-stretch feedback and inform the design of scalable proprioceptive haptic feedback interfaces for assistive robots.

title: A Real-Time Go-Based Active Scraper for OEM Vulnerability Detection in Converged IT–OT Environments
authors: Manisha More, Om Hase, Mayur Ingale, Amit Holkar, Harshal Dhotre
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence &amp; Networking (QPAIN)
abstract: The convergence of Information Technology (IT) and Operational Technology (OT) has significantly expanded the industrial attack surface, making traditional manual tracking of Original Equipment Manufacturer (OEM) advisories increasingly inadequate. This paper presents a high-performance research prototype designed for the automated extraction, normalization, and analysis of security advisories from diverse OEMs, including Cisco, Juniper Networks, and Honeywell. At the core of the framework is a Go-based concurrent engine that utilizes lightweight goroutines to achieve high-throughput data acquisition, yielding an 80 % reduction in collection latency compared to traditional single threaded frameworks. The prototype features a programmable polling architecture, allowing users to define specific monitoring intervals such as <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0}$</tex> or <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 5}$</tex> minutes based on the criticality of the target industrial environment. Beyond simple data collection, this research introduces a unified normalization schema to transform heterogeneous, unstructured vendor alerts into a structured threat intelligence format. While the prototype achieves <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{9 5 \%}$</tex> classification accuracy through automated severity mapping, it primarily serves to bridge the intelligence gap left by general-purpose scanners. This research validates that modern concurrency primitives and systematic data normalization are essential for maintaining a resilient and proactive security posture within increasingly complex and converged industrial ecosystems.

title: SMART: Scalable Motion-Adaptive Robust Tracking in Complex Motion Scenarios
authors: Zijing Zhao, Xiang Wei, Zhihui Li, Xiaojun Chang, Shunli Zhang
abstract: Multi-Object Tracking (MOT) detects multiple objects in videos and assigns unique identities across consecutive frames, generating complete motion tracks. Existing methods typically associate fixed-size predicted tracks with detections above a threshold, but objects with complex motion patterns, such as sudden changes in speed or direction, often cause motion estimation errors, leading to missed associations and fragmented tracks. To tackle this, we analyze the impact of attributes like speed, size, and detection confidence on tracking performance. Based on this, we propose an adaptive scaling strategy that dynamically adjusts the size of predicted tracks to create adaptively scaled bounding boxes. These bounding boxes enable a Motion-Adaptive IoU (MA-IoU) association strategy for more robust matching between detections and predicted tracks. Additionally, we introduce a self-supervised training method to optimize the adaptive scaling network and MA-IoU across diverse scenarios. Finally, we cascade MA-IoU to develop SMART (Scalable Motion-Adaptive Robust Tracking), a robust and efficient multi-object tracker. SMART achieves state-of-the-art results on BEE24 and DanceTrack. On the BEE24 benchmark, SMART achieves 67.2 MOTA, 63.4 IDF1, and 47.9 HOTA, demonstrating superior performance in handling objects with complex motion and indistinguishable appearances. This comprehensive approach addresses the limitations of prior methods, offering a significant advancement in multi-object tracking.

title: Fine-Tuning Pre-Trained Faster R-CNN to Reduce False Positives in Guardrail Damage Detection from Dashcam Images
authors: S. T. Jones, A. H. Le, R. Raj, D. Sacharny, Z. Al-Halah, T. C. Henderson, S. Kundur
publish: 2026 IEEE Southwest Symposium on Image Analysis and Interpretation (SSIAI)
abstract: Highway guardrails are critical safety features that require timely maintenance to remain effective. However, current inspection methods are manual, reactive, costly, and prone to human error, often failing to detect damage from/due to unreported incidents. We develop a deep learning finetuning pipeline to reduce false positives for automated guardrail damage detection using dashcam images implemented via supervised fine-tuning (SFT) of a pre-trained Faster R-CNN model using focal loss to address class imbalance and improve sensitivity to subtle damage features. We further evaluate the impact of data augmentation, custom anchor box design, two-stage detection, and post-processing techniques, such as DBSCAN clustering and Soft Non-Maximum Suppression (SoftNMS). Our results demonstrate that SFT with focal loss reduces false positives (from 2.84 to 0.25 per image) while maintaining precision and recall. This work presents a scalable and effective approach for guardrail damage detection, with potential applications in other infrastructure monitoring systems for improved roadway safety.

title: t-SNE-Based Analysis of Brain State Dynamics During Reading of AI vs Human Writing
authors: Harshit Parmar, Eric Walden
publish: 2026 IEEE Southwest Symposium on Image Analysis and Interpretation (SSIAI)
abstract: Traditional fMRI analyses rely on static block or event related experiment design that captures average brain activation but are limited in characterizing dynamic neural processes during continuous naturalistic tasks. Building on our previously validated t-SNE based brain state mapping framework, we apply this method to examine temporal neural dynamics while participants read essays written either by professional human writers or large language model. The t-SNE embedding transforms whole-brain activity into a low-dimensional trajectory, revealing moment-to-moment state transitions without predefined task boundaries. AI essays produced more frequent and synchronized brain state transitions across readers (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{t}=\mathbf{- 2. 2 6}$</tex>, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{p}=.024$</tex>), whereas human essays evoked fewer, more individualized transitions. Lexical segmentation analyses showed that t-SNE detected brain-state changes aligned with lexical boundaries, indicating that the identified dynamics reflected meaningful linguistic structure. Complementary conventional GLM and MVPA supported these findings, showing stronger semantic and social-cognitive activation for human writing. Together, the results demonstrate that t-SNE enables dynamic analysis of naturalistic fMRI data, revealing temporal organization in complex, real-world tasks.

title: A Novel Notched-Gate LDMOS for Robust Reliability via Electric Field Modulation and Low Gate Charge on 55-nm BCD Platform
authors: Dingxiang Ma, Hanwen Bai, Fanyi Zeng, Jiawei Wang, Yangjie Liao, Bo Zhang, Ming Qiao
publish: 2026 IEEE 38th International Symposium on Power Semiconductor Devices and ICs (ISPSD)
abstract: This paper presents a novel notched-gate LDMOS (NG LDMOS) featuring partial removal of poly above the step oxide (SOX) to achieve low gate charge (Q<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">G</inf>) performance. The robustness of NG LDMOS under hot-carrier degradation (HCD) and high-temperature reverse bias (HTRB) is improved by modulating on-state impact-ionization and the off-state surface electric field distribution. Demonstrated through 3D simulations with an appropriate 40nm SOX thickness, this improvement is achieved by optimizing the poly finger space width. Moreover, the 8V-class NG LDMOS is fabricated on a standard 55-nm BCD platform without additional masks. In the experiment, the measured results of NG LDMOS achieve a 15% reduction in Q<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">G</inf> compared to the conventional LDMOS and maintain comparable R<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">SP</inf> (1.98 mΩ·mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>) and BV (17.4 V). Importantly, the optimized NG LDMOS achieves an excellent figure of merit (R<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">SP</inf>•Q<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">G</inf>) of 11.94 mΩ·nC while demonstrating comparable reliability to conventional LDMOS in both HCD and HTRB experiments.

title: A Proposed Tuning Algorithm for Closed-Loop VFDs to Reduce Input Current THD Using LC Filtering
authors: Md. Mehedi Hasan, Fardin Ahmed, Md. Asif Hasan, Md. Shahadat Shahriar Shaon
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence &amp; Networking (QPAIN)
abstract: This paper presents a proposed tuning algorithm for a closed-loop variable frequency drive (VFD) aimed at input current THD reduction through an optimized LC filter design. In modern industrial applications, nonlinear switching in VFDs often results in excessive harmonic propagation, which reduces power quality and increases power loss. The proposed method focused on adaptive LC filter design and controller tuning, resulting in a significant reduction of THD compared to existing open-loop and unfiltered methods. The algorithm determines optimal resonance frequency and damping factor based on real-time operating conditions, ensuring effective reduction in THD across a wide range of loads. Simulation results demonstrate the proposed approach in THD reduction while maintaining steady-state performance and fast transient response. Compared with the simulation result, THD can be further reduced using the proposed algorithm and concludes with a THD of 1.82%

title: Variational Bayesian Joint Measurement Anomaly and Noise Inference Filter for Robust Cellular/MEMS-INS Localization
authors: Xumin Feng, Tian Jin, Honglei Qin
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: In Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSS)-denied urban environments, cellular/microelectromechanical system-inertial navigation system (MEMS-INS) integrated positioning provides a promising solution for continuous navigation. However, in such environments, signal propagation is severely affected by multipath, blockage, and environmental dynamics, leading to heterogeneous measurement uncertainties, including time-varying noise, impulsive outliers, and persistent NLOS biases, which are difficult to handle within conventional estimation frameworks. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a Variational Bayesian Joint Measurement Anomaly and Noise Inference Filter (VB-JMANIF) for robust positioning. The proposed method explicitly models NLOS-induced biases, impulsive outliers, and time-varying measurement noise, and jointly integrates anomaly classification, bias estimation, noise adaptation, and state update within an error-state cubature Kalman filter (CKF) framework. Simulation results demonstrate that VB-JMANIF consistently outperforms the conventional CKF and several state-of-the-art robust and noise-adaptive methods in terms of positioning accuracy, estimation stability, and robustness. Field experiments using real cellular signals further validate the effectiveness of the proposed approach, achieving horizontal positioning Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) values of 4.7 m and 6.1 m under moderate and severely degraded urban measurement conditions, respectively, thereby demonstrating its practical applicability in real-world environments.

title: Bayesian Wind Forecast Correction for Real-Time CVaR-Based UAV Path Replanning Using Onboard Telemetry
authors: Ferdus Hossain, Abu Shahed Shah Md Nazmul Arefin, Trishan Talukder, Abdullah Al Rafe Ayon
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence &amp; Networking (QPAIN)
abstract: Public weather APIs provide wind forecasts accessible for pre-mission UAV trajectory planning. But systematic biases of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2-5 \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$</tex> are commonly found in tropical coastal environments, directly impacting energy efficiency and safety. In this paper, a closed-loop system is proposed that incorporates IMU and airspeed sensor measurements with Bayesian forecast correction for real-time update of Conditional Value-at-Risk (CVaR) and trajectory planning. Using an Extended Kalman Filter (EKF), forecast bias and variance are addressed at each UAV waypoint. If the updated <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{CVaR}_{0.95}$</tex> exceeds a threshold, warm start RRT* is utilized for trajectory planning within computational constraints. The proposed method is evaluated using a 1.94 km Sundarbans mission with synthetic wind data with known bias, achieving <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{7 2. 0 \%}$</tex> RMSE reduction (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{2. 0 4} \rightarrow \mathbf{0. 5 7 m} / \mathbf{s}$</tex>), 12.6 % CVaR risk reduction, and 12.0 % energy savings versus the uncorrected baseline. EKF bias estimation achieves 79.6 % accuracy within five waypoints, ensuring reliable correction for the rest of the mission.

title: A Compact and Reliable ACT Gate Driving Scheme Based on Active Clamp Paradigm
authors: Fucheng Liu, Jinpeng Wu, Chunpin Ren, Hui Liu, Han Wang, Lin Dong, Xiangyu Zhao, Qingtao Meng, Jiapeng Liu
abstract: The advent of Avalanche Controlled Thyristor (ACT) has addressed the shortage of the turn-off capability of Integrated Gate Commutated Thyristor (IGCT), establishing it as a promising candidate for DC circuit breaker (DCCB). However, its practical application is constrained by the complexity of the gate driver, which conventionally necessitates an additional blocking module for the blocking state. This article proposes a novel ACT gate driving scheme based on active clamp paradigm. By reconfiguring the turn-off module to provide the blocking function, the proposed topology eliminates the need for an additional blocking module. A 4.5 kV ACT prototype was developed to validate the scheme. Experimental results confirm that the proposed driver maintains same functionalities of conventional ACT. Furthermore, the proposed scheme reduces the driver footprint by 18% and static power loss by 15% while offering a more favorable reliability basis than the conventional scheme. These attributes position the proposed scheme as a promising solution for DCCB applications.

title: PELS Membership
authors: N/A

title: Volumetric Near-Field Electromagnetic Sensing of Creatinine in Urine Using a 3D-Printed Bifilar Helical Platform
authors: Rayan Al-Sayed Ali, Joseph Costantine, Youssef Tawk, Rouwaida Kanj, Joanna Ghaddar, Fatima Asadallah, Nassim Fares, Dania Chelala, Assaad A. Eid
abstract: This paper presents a microwave sensing platform for quantitative evaluation of creatinine concentration in urine using a 3D-printed bifilar helical resonant structure. The proposed sensing system integrates two conductive helices with a slotted cylindrical shell incorporating volumetric split-ring resonators, enabling enhanced confinement of near-field electromagnetic energy within the sensing region. This design strengthens the interaction between the electromagnetic fields and the urine sample, resulting in increased sensitivity to variations in creatinine concentration. The sensing structure is fabricated using precision additive manufacturing to ensure high reproducibility and stable electromagnetic performance. Operating in the 4–6.5 GHz frequency range, the sensor response is characterized through measured scattering parameters, which are processed using artificial intelligence techniques for both regression and classification tasks. Gaussian Process regression is employed to estimate creatinine concentration, achieving a mean absolute relative difference (MARD) of 10% for artificial urine samples, between 6.94% and 10% for ex-vivo rat studies, and 7.2% for clinical human urine samples. In addition, a support vector machine classifier is used to detect creatinine in the presence of common interferers, achieving an accuracy ranging from 92.5% to 97.5%, a sensitivity between 90% and 100%, and a specificity between 91.6% and 100%. The proposed platform demonstrates a robust, reagent-free, and noninvasive approach for real-time creatinine monitoring, offering promising potential for future point-of-care diagnostic applications and early assessment of kidney function.

title: Post-Die-Bond Defect Detection in Semiconductor Packaging AOI Under Extreme Data Scarcity: Copy-Paste Augmentation and Leakage-Aware Evaluation
authors: Der-Fa Chen, Yanyu Lai, Chih-Chung Wang
abstract: In this study, we target the early-production bootstrap stage of an automatic optical inspection system, where a new inspection station or production line has started collecting data but true defect cases remain extremely scarce.We evaluated same-station defect localization at an inspection station between die bonding and wire bonding in a semiconductor packaging line. After exact image-level deduplication, the dataset contained 135 images, including 21 defective images and 30 annotated boxes. Because one physical defect could appear in more than one camera view, these annotations corresponded to 19 independent physical defects across 14 die locations. The practical question is whether a first useful defect-localization model can be built before enough defective samples accumulate for conventional supervised training. The experiments used a standard one-stage detector, offline Copy-Paste augmentation, and leakage-aware grouped cross-validation that kept repeated views of the same physical defect in the same fold. Under three random seeds with five folds per seed, the raw baseline reached a mean Average Precision at 0.50 IoU (mAP50) of 0.563, while the CP80× setting reached 0.957 with recall 0.935. As an anomaly-detection reference, PatchCore reached an image-level area under the receiver operating characteristic curve of 0.977 ± 0.020 for separating defect-free and defective images, but it does not produce three-class bounding boxes. These results indicate that fold-local data construction and leakage-aware validation can make same-station early detector bootstrapping practical when defects are rare, while station-specific background regularity and the absence of cross-station validation remain external-validity limitations.

title: QANet: A Neural Framework for Quality Assessment of Instance Segmentation in Microscopy Imaging
authors: Amit Aharoni, Assaf Arbelle, Michael Sidorov, Eliav Elul, Tammy Riklin Raviv
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Signal Processing
abstract: Reliable instance segmentation of cells in microscopy images is essential for quantitative imaging analysis, yet estimating segmentation quality on unseen data, though fundamental, remains largely overlooked. In practice, performance assessment typically requires manual ground-truth annotations, which are costly and impractical at scale. We address this evaluation gap by introducing QANet, a neural framework for post-hoc quality assessment of instance segmentations. Unlike segmentation models, QANet does not predict masks; instead, it receives an image and a segmentation produced by any method and estimates a quantitative quality score that approximates standard evaluation metrics, without requiring ground-truth annotations at inference time. QANet is model-agnostic and formulated as a regression task over image–mask pairs. It is built on the RibCage architecture, which performs multi-scale comparison between image content and segmentation structure, enabling sensitivity to both global shape consistency and fine boundary errors. Training is performed using synthetically perturbed segmentations with known quality scores, enabling supervision across controlled error modes while eliminating the need for large annotated failure datasets. We evaluate QANet on 2D and 3D datasets from the Cell Segmentation Benchmark and demonstrate accurate prediction of both overlap-based measures and boundary-sensitive metrics across multiple segmentation methods. The code is available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/amita1996/QANet</uri>.

title: Optimizing Probability Breast Cancer Diagnosis based Random Forest using Ant Colony Algorithm
authors: Yahya Nur Ifriza, Daffa Ra'if Ridhodin, Subhan Subhan, Devi Ajeng Efrilianda
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence &amp; Networking (QPAIN)
abstract: Breast cancer is one of the most common diseases that affect women and needs to be correctly diagnosed as soon as possible. Using machine learning in medical decision support systems is one potential method to diagnose this illness more accurately. This study sought to address the limitations of classification techniques like Random Forest through the selection of pertinent characteristics. This study focuses on improving feature selection by utilizing the Ant Colony Optimization (ACO) method, aiming to boost the accuracy of breast cancer detection. This work also advances the development of machine learning-based medical decision support systems and addresses the need for a more precise and effective classification model. The dataset utilized was the Wisconsin Diagnostic Breast Cancer (WDBC) from the UCI Machine Learning Repository. Relevant traits were identified through ACO prior to categorization with Random Forest algorithms. Evaluation is based on classification accuracy. The Random Forest algorithm's classification accuracy increased from 96.49% to 99.12% after feature selection using ACO. ACO was also able to reduce the number of characteristics and maximize computational performance. This investigation employs Random Forest alongside a metaheuristic optimization method known as Ant Colony Optimization to enhance the model's precision and effectiveness in identifying breast cancer.

title: Faster R-CNN-Based Fracture Localization in Radiographs Using a Resnet-50 Backbone
authors: Md. Raihan Mahamud, Sumaiya Rahman, Md. Jarez Miah
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence &amp; Networking (QPAIN)
abstract: Accurate localization of bone fractures in radiographs is essential for improving diagnostic efficiency and clinical decision-making. This paper presents a Faster R-CNN-based fracture localization framework employing a ResNet-50 backbone with a Feature Pyramid Network (FPN) to detect and localize fracture regions using bounding boxes. The model is trained on a curated dataset of annotated X-ray images following a COCO-style format with a single foreground class named fracture. Training was conducted across 50 epochs using the AdamW optimizer with a cosine-annealing learning-rate schedule, and ImageNet normalization. The configurationdriven pipeline automatically manages logging, checkpointing, and validation tracking, ensuring full reproducibility. The smooth reduction of both training and validation losses with increasing the number of epochs confirms consistent learning dynamics with minimal overfitting. Quantitative results demonstrate stable convergence and strong localization capability, achieving a validation accuracy of 0.9, F1-score of 0.7, precision of 0.8, recall of 0.7, and intersection-over-union (IoU) of 0.81. These findings indicate that the ResNet-50-based Faster R-CNN provides a highly accurate, interpretable, and computationally feasible approach for fracture localization in radiographic images, offering a promising foundation for computer-aided diagnostic systems and future multi-backbone comparative studies.

title: Dual-Mode Linear Cross-Polarization Converter-Based Highly Sensitive Refractive Index Sensor_supp1-3700132.docx
authors: SARTHAK SINGHAL
abstract: A multiarm Z-shaped resonator and slot-based wideband dual-mode terahertz (THz) linear cross-polarization converter (LCPC) for refractive index-based biosensing applications is proposed. It has a unitcell volume of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$0.134\lambda _{\mathrm {L}} \times 0.093\lambda _{\mathrm {L}} \times 0.061\lambda _{\mathrm {L}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\lambda _{\mathrm {L}} =148.5$</tex-math> </inline-formula> <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>m) while exhibiting reflective polarization conversion ratio (PCR<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{\mathrm {rx}}) \ge 80$</tex-math> </inline-formula>% across 2.02–6.59 THz and transmissive polarization conversion ratio (PCR<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{\mathrm {tx}}) \ge 80$</tex-math> </inline-formula>% across the spectrum of 3.67–3.91 THz and 6.43–7.29 THz. Physical phenomenon behind the polarization conversions is studied through electric field distribution. It has stable PCR<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{\mathrm {rx}} \ge 80$</tex-math> </inline-formula>% performance for incidence angles up to 30°. Its maximal sensitivity (S<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{\max }$</tex-math> </inline-formula>) as a refractive index sensor is 618 GHz/RIU using PCR<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">rx</sub> and 792 GHz/RIU using PCR<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">tx</sub>. Its biosensor configuration has a S<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">max</sub> of 4.95 THz/RIU (PCR<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{\mathrm {tx}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>) and 16.76 THz/RIU (PCR<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{\mathrm {rx}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>). The proposed polarization converter’s wideband performance, dual-mode sensing capability, high sensitivity, and compact dimensions indicate that it is suitable for a variety of applications, such as wireless communication, biosensing, imaging, and security screening.

title: A GaN Device with Self-Generated Negative Voltage for Enhanced Turn-Off Reliability
authors: Xinyang Liu, Rongxing Lai, Yun Dai, Xudong Li, Wen Xiao, Wenhong He, Zekun Zhou, Bo Zhang, Gaofei Tang, Wen Shi, Zhiwen Dong, Qimeng Jiang
publish: 2026 IEEE 38th International Symposium on Power Semiconductor Devices and ICs (ISPSD)
abstract: This paper presents a novel self-generated negative voltage turn-off Gallium Nitride (SelfNeg-GaN) device. It features a power-free negative voltage charge pump monolithically integrated into a power GaN transistor, requiring only a small co-packaged capacitor to form a standard 3-terminal discrete GaN device. The SelfNeg-GaN generates a negative voltage pulse at its internal gate during turn-off, effectively suppressing false turn-on and ringing while avoiding prolonged negative gate bias. Fabricated using a 650 V 0.5 µm E-GaN process, the device maintains the static characteristics of conventional GaN devices and achieves a peak negative gate voltage of -4.4 V with a duration of 265 ns during turn-off. These advantages are further validated through double-pulse testing, confirming the device’s superior turn-off reliability.

title: CardioGuard: An Intelligent System for Early Heart Disease Prediction
authors: Sadia Sultana Maisha, Hifjun Nahar Nabilah, Arifuzzaman
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence &amp; Networking (QPAIN)
abstract: One of the main causes of death in the globe is heart disease, and accurate prediction remains a major challenge for medical practitioners due to the complexity of clinical data. Although the healthcare sector generates large volumes of data, effective analytical tools for extracting meaningful patterns are often lacking. Using feature selection and ensemble approaches, this work suggests an intelligent machine learning-based system for early heart disease prediction. Several models were trained and evaluated, including Random Forest, XG Boost, Cat Boost, and Logistic Regression. Among them, the Random Forest model achieved the highest accuracy of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{8 9. 1 3 \%}$</tex>, while Logistic Regression achieved 87.50 %. The findings show that predictive performance can be greatly enhanced by combining tailored clinical features with ensemble learning. The goal of the suggested system is to offer an effective, precise, and comprehensive method for early identification of heart disease.

title: A Large Language Model-Driven Reinforcement Learning Framework for Intelligent Employee Performance Optimization
authors: Md. Ferdaus Hossen, Md. Abdur Rahman, Md Raihan Alam Rahi, Gourab Kanti Singha, Hazrat Ali, Rafatul Islam, Md. Zahidul Hasan, Mehedi Hasan Shuvo
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence &amp; Networking (QPAIN)
abstract: This paper presents a hybrid LLM-assisted Reinforcement Learning framework for intelligent employee performance management that combines structured HR metrics with contextual semantic feedback. The system lets people make decisions about workload allocation, task optimization, and performance forecasting in real time by using Meta-RL for quick adaptation and LLM embeddings for understanding behavior in a qualitative way. Experimental results show that KPI enhancement, learning efficiency, and employee well-being metrics have all improved a lot compared to traditional RL and supervised baselines. The suggested method gives modern businesses that want adaptive and ethically aligned workforce management systems a solution that can grow, is easy to understand, and works quickly.

title: Integrated Multi-Modal Ensemble (IMME) for Zero-Day Attack Detection in IIoT
authors: Abdullah Rakib Akand, Md. Sazzadur Rahman
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence &amp; Networking (QPAIN)
abstract: The rapid proliferation of Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) devices has introduced significant security vulnerabilities, particularly from zero-day attacks that traditional signature-based systems fail to detect. This paper proposes a novel unsupervised ensemble method, the Integrated Multi-Modal Ensemble (IMME), for robust zero-day attack detection in IIoT environments. The ensemble integrates five distinct anomaly detectors: Adaptive Streaming Clustering, Autoencoder (AE) reconstruction error, Mahalanobis distance in latent space, Isolation Forest (IF), and an O(1) Sliding-Window Rarity Kernel. Evaluation using the CICIoT 2023 dataset demonstrates that IMME achieves a high ROC-AUC of 0.9560, an Unknown Detection Rate (UDR) of 47 %, and an Open-Set Classification Rate (OSCR) of 47.5 %, while maintaining 100 % precision through a validation-optimized thresholding approach. The system exhibits a throughput of 79,000 flows/sec, proving its viability for real-time IIoT deployment.

title: Deep Ensemble Learning for Early Crops Leaf Disease Classification and Risk Reduction
authors: Dip Kumar Saha, Tuhin Hossain, Jahidul Islam, Tushar Deb Nath
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence &amp; Networking (QPAIN)
abstract: Plant diseases can significantly impact agricultural productivity and jeopardize a sufficient global supply of food. Therefore, early plant disease diagnosis reduces risks and losses. However, identifying and classifying plant leaf diseases by hand has become challenging and time-intensive, and raises the chance of farmer error. On the contrary, deep learning (DL) algorithms have demonstrated impressive outcomes when it comes to the early detection of diseases in plants. This study introduces a novel approach for categorizing plant leaf diseases to alleviate these limitations. The proposed approach creates a state-of-the-art ensemble model using Xception, MobilenetV3, and EfficientNetB7. This ensemble model incorporates a three-layer pre-train model that utilizes a coordinate attention block, softmax layer, reshape layers, dense layer, flattened layer, and a 60 % dropout rate. To enhance the model performance, we employed data augmentation techniques like horizontal flips along with histogram equalization. In addition, this research evaluated the model performance with and without transfer learning (TL) and augmentation. Impressively, our proposed ensemble model, incorporating TL and augmentation, outperformed existing models with 98.70 % accuracy, 99.10 % precision, 98.70 % recall, and 98.80 % F1 score in classifying images of both normal and diseased leaves. Experimental results showed that our identification models adequately prevented agricultural diseases and ensured food security. Additionally, modern explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) utilizing gradient-weighted class activation mapping (Grad-CAM) can precisely identify crop-affected regions.

title: Guessing-Flipping Decoding Framework for Short Polar Codes in 6G
authors: Xuanyu Li, Yuxin Han, Kai Niu, Bolin Wu, Jiankui Yang
abstract: The guessing random additive noise decoding (GRAND) algorithm is known for its maximum likelihood performance and high throughput, yet there remains potential for optimization in specific coding schemes, as GRAND is only suitable for high-rate codes. For this, we introduce the guessing-flipping (GF) decoding framework, where error patterns (EPs) are continuously used to flip the most unreliable bits like GRAND, but an extra baseline decoder, like successive cancellation with list (SCL), is utilized on the flipped signal to determine if the correct codeword is obtained or another EP should be applied. The simulation results demonstrate that the GF-SCL decoder achieves improved performance and low complexity, practical in 6G traffic and control channels.

title: Multi-Class Kidney Disease Classification from CT Images Using Explainable Deep Learning
authors: Arr Rafi, Abdullah Omar Nasif, Md Nahiduzzaman, Muhammad Nazrul Islam
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence &amp; Networking (QPAIN)
abstract: Kidney diseases such as cysts, stones, and tumors affect a large number of the population worldwide, and making an accurate diagnosis is often time-consuming for medical imaging. This paper proposes an explainable deep learning approach for multi-class classification of kidney conditions using CT images. We used a publicly available dataset of 12,446 grayscale CT images, which have four categories: Normal, Cyst, Tumor, and Stone. Five convolutional neural network architectures (VGG16, VGG19, ResNet50, DenseNet121, InceptionResNetV2) are finetuned using transfer learning and comprehensive data augmentation. We also integrate Grad-CAM heatmap visualizations to explain model decisions. In experiments, the VGG19 model gets a test accuracy of 99.46%, and InceptionResNetV2 gets 98.0%. The results show excellent performance and stability. The explainability analysis highlights the relevant kidney regions that influence each prediction. Our findings indicate that explainable deep learning is a promising tool for the detection of kidney disease from CT scans.

title: Face Recognition Using Pre-Trained CNN Embeddings and a Custom Deep Neural Network
authors: MD Iftakhar Kabir Sakur, Ahmed Musa Chowdhury, Abdullah Al Noman, Md Jiabul Haque, S. M. Shahidullah Kaiser Talib, Md Arfatul Islam
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence &amp; Networking (QPAIN)
abstract: Facial recognition technology has seen rapid advancements and development, especially with the adoption of deep learning algorithms. In this method, a custom Deep Neural Network (DNN) model is trained on two different datasets. This study also focuses on a video-based facial recognition system to efficiently and accurately recognize any specific person who is in the dataset. There is also shown the comparison with Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), along with traditional methods like Eigenface, Fisherface, and Local Binary Patterns Histogram (LBPH), as well as other deep learning approaches like Support Vector Machines (SVM) and Multilayer Perceptron (MLP). This study achieved state-of-the-art accuracy of 94 % on a larger dataset with 5,817 labels from online and 99.37 % on a self-collected smaller dataset with 68 labels and 1,564 augmented samples. These findings highlight the custom DNN model's suitability for real-world facial recognition applications, offering high accuracy and robustness across different datasets.

title: Explainable Deep Learning for Accurate Multi-Crop Leaf Disease Classification in Intelligent Agricultural Systems
authors: Sonet Barua Emon, Mithila Arman, Atahar Hossain, Golam Ali, Moshiur Rahman Sayem
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence &amp; Networking (QPAIN)
abstract: Identifying crop leaf diseases accurately and in an automated fashion is pivotal for advancing agricultural productivity and tackling global food challenges. This study utilizes an explainable deep learning approach for multi-crop leaf disease classification incorporating five benchmark architectures VGG16, ResNet, EfficientNet, Inception, and Vision Transformer (ViT). The models' performance is evaluated using an extensive dataset of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{5 5, 8 2 4}$</tex> images, covering 28 classes of diseases from five fruit crops. For each image, resizing, normalization, and augmentation were applied as preprocessing strategies to promote generalization. Among the models, the VGG16 network attained the highest classification accuracy of 97.68% and the highest F1-score of 0.977, surpassing ResNet (96.2 %) and EfficientNet (96.5 %). In comparison, ViT and Inception relied more on data and were lower performing at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{8 0. 0 6 \%}$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{5 1. 2 9 \%}$</tex> accuracy, respectively. The explainable aspects of the framework were validated using grad-CAM techniques which localized the affected disease areas, assuring agricultural practitioners of the framework's reliability. These findings explain why less sophisticated convolutional networks were able to deliver better performance than complex transformer networks for tasks involving agricultural images. The proposed explainable model offers a scalable and interpretable solution for early disease detection, supporting precision farming initiatives.

title: Short Time-scale Analysis of Dead-time Effect in Dual Active Half-Bridge Converter
authors: Su-Bin Kang, Hyeon-Sik Kim
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: In this article, the dead-time effects of a dual active half-bridge (DAHB) converter are analyzed on a short time scale. Dead time distorts the ac-side voltages of both bridges, leading to polarity reversal and phase drift. Consequently, power transfer characteristics depend not only on the phase-shift ratio and voltage ratio but also on the dead-time value. A detailed short time-scale analysis of the operating waveforms is presented over one switching cycle, incorporating various operating states such as buck, match, and boost conditions. From this analysis, precise power flow equations are derived, revealing that the power characteristics become non-monotonic and exhibit power plateau regions depending on the operating modes. Furthermore, a dead-time compensation algorithm is proposed to mitigate these distortions and linearize the output voltage control. Finally, the validity of the derived model is confirmed through simulation and experimental results under extensive power flow conditions.

title: AI Driven Multimodal Arrival Time Prediction for Urban Autorickshaw and Bus Networks Using GPS Telemetry and Demand–Supply Analytics
authors: Sundarakrishnan Narayanan, Tanmay Umesh, Sohan Varier, Tarun Bhupathi, Mohana
abstract: This paper presents a unified system for predicting urban travel times across buses and auto-rickshaws in Bengaluru, with an emphasis on real-time accuracy and smooth multimodal travel. The work covers three linked tasks: forecasting when a bus will reach its upcoming stops, estimating auto-rickshaw ride durations, and combining both to compute end-to-end auto–bus–auto journeys. Thisworkwas developed as a part of The Bengaluru Last Mile Challenge 2025 in collaboration with the Indian Institute of Science, BMTC and Namma Yatri.The bus component processes large volumes of GPS telemetry across 7509 BMTC bus routes in the city.
