title: Discrete-Time Value Iteration-Based Position Tracking Control for Electro-Hydraulic Systems With Unmeasured States
authors: Ashok Kumar Kumawat, Raja Rout, Pramila
abstract: novel infinite-horizon linear quadratic tracking (LQT) problem for the discrete-time system with unmeasured states is presented in this paper. The reference trajectory dynamics and the electro-hydraulic system are combined to create an augmented system. A new Bellman equation is derived to evaluate the value function associated with the devised policy. The LQT problem of the augmented system dynamics is solved by adaptive dynamic programming. Herein, a discrete-time value iteration (VI) algorithm is proposed that generates a control signal for the proportional directional control (PDC) valve, satisfying the actuation limits of [-10V, 10V]. To confirm the efficacy of the proposed control approach, three experiments utilizing square wave and multi-step reference trajectories are shown. The experimental findings verify that tracking error converges to an arbitrarily tiny neighborhood and that the electro-hydraulic actuator system’s tracking performance is enhanced.

title: Leveraging a passive MRAM crossbar for hardware-in-the-loop and continual learning_supp1-3702001.pdf
authors: D. RAVELOSONA

title: TrojanGPL: Vulnerability Analysis and Mitigation of Backdoor Attacks on Graph Prompt Learning
authors: Xiaoting Lyu, Yufei Han, Hangwei Qian, Ivor W. Tsang, Wei Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: Graph Prompt Learning (GPL) effectively bridges the gap between graph pre-training and downstream tasks, addressing the bottleneck in knowledge transfer. However, its susceptibility to backdoor attacks during pre-training remains largely unexplored. This work systematically investigates the backdoor risks in GPL and introduces TrojanGPL-a cross-context backdoor attack that embeds triggers solely through manipulations of the pre-trained encoder, independent of downstream knowledge. Our analysis reveals that jointly optimizing trigger graphs with the prompt mechanism not only narrows the gap between upstream and downstream main tasks but also aligns main and backdoor learning objectives, facilitating seamless backdoor transfer from pre-trained models to diverse downstream applications. To counter this threat, we propose ES-Detector, a defense mechanism that identifies backdoored graph samples by detecting discriminative embedding shifts upon the removal of important nodes. Specifically, when clean and backdoored graphs are predicted as the same class, clean graphs exhibit stable embedding changes, whereas backdoored graphs show significant deviations from clean ones. Extensive experiments on 5 benchmark datasets and 3 GPL frameworks demonstrate that TrojanGPL achieves an attack success rate exceeding 90% in different cross-context scenarios while minimally impacting the performance of the main task. Meanwhile, ES-Detector reduces the attack success rate by at least 60% across all the test settings, validating its practical robustness without prior knowledge of attack patterns.

title: Study on the Peeling Mechanism of Aged Layer Removal from Silicone Rubber Sheds Using Pulsed Laser
authors: Yulin Wang, Yuyao Hu, Xingliang Jiang, Xuening Xing, Dongdong Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: Silicone rubber possesses excellent anti-pollution flashover performance and insulation properties, making it widely used as a shed material for composite insulators. With increasing service time, silicone rubber materials gradually degrade under the influence of adverse factors such as ultraviolet radiation and salt spray, leading to the formation of an aged layer on the surface. To remove the aged layer from the silicone rubber surface without damaging the substrate, this study elucidates the peeling mechanism of pulsed laser on silicone rubber through simulation and experimental validation. The peeling process of silicone rubber by pulsed laser is accomplished through the combined effects of thermal ablation and stress field. In the initial stage of laser action, the surface temperature of the silicone rubber rises sharply, dominated by the thermal ablation mechanism. In the middle and later stages, the surface temperature stabilizes, while the stress field gradually propagates into the depth of the silicone rubber and increases in magnitude, at this point, the process is governed by the combined action of thermal ablation and the stress field. Both laser fluence and the number of scans are positively correlated with the peeling depth. After five scans, the contact angle on the aged layer surface of the silicone rubber reaches 142°–156°, the atomic percent ratio of Si and C elements increase by 2.1%–3.4% and 5.1%–10.9%, respectively, while the O element content decreases by 7.1%–14.2%.

title: A General Analysis of Passive Component Sizing for Current-Sourced Buck Converters
authors: Qijia Li, Jonah Rawley, Samantha Coday
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Power Electronics
abstract: Power converter topologies with inductors at the input offer the potential to reduce conducted electromagnetic interference (EMI). To harness this benefit in converters conventionally implemented with inductors at the output, an input passive network consisting of two inductors and a bypass capacitor can be utilized instead of the output inductor. This work focuses on the current-sourced buck converter (CSBC), the most fundamental two input inductor topology, and presents a comprehensive analytical model for the bypass capacitor voltage and input inductor currents. In addition, an analytical expression for the output voltage is derived, demonstrating its dependence on not only the duty ratio but also the switching and resonant frequencies of the CSBC. The analysis is expanded from an uncoupled CSBC to include magnetic coupling between the input inductors, which enables the CSBC to be implemented with only one magnetic core. The effects of coupling are thoroughly analyzed, including the potential to reduce passive component volume. The proposed model is validated through simulation and experimental results for a design example. Design insights are provided to guide passive component value selection, evaluate switch stress based on capacitor voltage and inductor current ripples, and identify operating conditions where magnetic coupling may be beneficial in reducing passive component volume.

title: Hybrid Kinematic and Muscular Null Space for Simultaneous Control of Natural and Extra Degrees of Freedom
authors: Julien Rossato, Bernardo Brogi, Tommaso Lisini Baldi, Elodie Hinnekens, Domenico Prattichizzo, Andrea D’Avella
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Systems and Rehabilitation Engineering
abstract: Humans possess more degrees of freedom than are required to perform upper-limb tasks, giving rise to a body null space that can be exploited to extend natural control capabilities. Both kinematic and muscular null space control modalities have been shown to be feasible in prior studies; however, their relative performance has not yet been compared within the same participants and task. Moreover, their potential combined use for flexible control of supernumerary degrees of freedom (SuDoFs) remains unexplored. In this study, we compare kinematic and muscular null space control and investigate a hybrid modality that either combines both to control one SuDoF or uses them separately to control two SuDoFs simultaneously. We propose a novel implementation of kinematic and muscular null space extraction that enables their real-time integration within a unified control framework. Sixteen participants performed a reaching and orientation task in a virtual reality environment, controlling the rotation of a virtual elliptical cursor to match targets with varying orientations. Task difficulty was modulated through single-axis and dual-axis target rotations, as well as with a baseline condition requiring no rotation control. Participants completed the task using muscular, kinematic, and hybrid null space control modalities. Results showed that muscular control outperformed purely kinematic control for rotated targets. The hybrid modality significantly improved performance relative to kinematic control, mitigating performance degradation, and supported both combined single-SuDoF control and simultaneous two-SuDoF control. These findings demonstrate that hybrid null space control is a robust and flexible strategy for human augmentation.

title: A Hybrid DSP–CNN Framework for Accurate Detection of Cardiac Abnormalities in ECG Signals
authors: Sudipta Saha, Md. Rakibul Hassan, Firoz Ahmed
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence &amp; Networking (QPAIN)
abstract: Cardiac abnormalities are a wide range of disorders that affects in the operational function of heart like arrhythmias, cardiomyopathy and heart failure. These conditions occurs a deep global health burden and cardiovascular diseases are one of them which is still the leading cause of death globally and it is responsible for millions of deaths every year. ECG signals provide a powerful visualization into the electrical activity of the heart that helps in the early detection of many abnormalities. To overcome this problem, this research has proposed a hybrid framework for the early detection of cardiac abnormalities in ECG signals which addressed the increasing global need for reliable early cardiac diagnosis. It proposes a hybrid framework that integrates digital signal processing techniques for effective noise reduction and feature extraction with a convolutional neural network for robust pattern recognition across physiological variations where it achieve 95% accuracy comparing to other models such as-Lstm, RNN, RestNet50.

title: Decentralized MNO Resource Management for B5G HetNets
authors: Faisal Zaman, Abdelhakim Senhaji Hafid, Dimitrios Makrakis, Abdallah Jarray
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Communications Society
abstract: Dynamic spectrum management in beyond-5G heterogeneous networks requires efficient inter-operator coordination under time-varying demand. Existing centralized solutions, such as Spectrum Access Systems (SAS), rely on heartbeat-based authorization and polling-driven updates. This introduces enforcement latency and transient spectrum under-utilization. These architectures also lack native support for real-time multi-operator spectrum transactions across licensed, shared, and unlicensed spectrum bands. In this paper, we propose a blockchain-enabled decentralized spectrum management framework that decouples local optimization from global coordination and policy enforcement, enabling event-driven spectrum transactions without centralized control. We model spectrum reconfiguration as a traffic-driven process where capacity shortfalls or cost-efficiency opportunities generate control transactions. The rate of these events defines the transaction density. We quantify coordination delay using spectrum reaction latency and transient capacity loss, and derive a break-even condition under which decentralized coordination outperforms centralized operation. Blockchain acts as a coordination layer that validates transactions, enforces constraints, and maintains a consistent global state, while optimization remains local to each operator. Simulation results across licensed, shared, and unlicensed bands show that decentralized coordination reduces reaction latency and transient loss, improving time-averaged utilization and cost under non-negligible delay. Gains are limited at low transaction density but increase as transaction density grows, establishing it as a key system design parameter.

title: Guest Editorial: Introduction to the Special Section on Near-Field Antenna Systems for Communications and Sensing
authors: Manuel Arrebola, Sérgio Matos, Álvaro F. Vaquero, Mauro Ettorre
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Antennas and Propagation

title: An Analytical Theory of Avalanche Breakdown in p-n Junctions Derived From Semiconductor Band Structure
authors: Huayou Liu, Jing Zhang, Abdelmadjid Mesli, Yaping Dan
abstract: This article presents a comprehensive analytical theory for the avalanche breakdown process in p-n junction diodes to address the critical reliance on empirical models in the design of avalanche photodiodes (APDs) and single-photon avalanche diodes (SPADs). The proposed model is derived from fundamental carrier continuity equations and impact excitation probability. Eventually, it yields closed-form expressions for the avalanche current density and the breakdown voltage. It demonstrates excellent agreement with experimental current–voltage characteristics across various semiconductors (Si, GaN, 4H-SiC, and InP) and a wide temperature range. TCAD simulations are also adopted to validate this model. The theory establishes a unified, physics-based criterion for defining the avalanche breakdown voltage, revealing its functional dependence on material parameters (bandgap, dielectric constant, and mean-free path) and doping concentrations. Significantly, it explicitly predicts the theoretical minimum and maximum limits for the breakdown voltage in a given material, which were well known but only implicitly reflected in previous empirical models. This work provides a rigorous theoretical framework for the steady-state dark avalanche current for the targeted design of diverse avalanche-based devices, ranging from low-breakdown-voltage SPADs for integrated quantum photonics to high-breakdown-voltage power devices, moving beyond trial-and-error approaches.

title: Phoenician Handwriting Alphabet Classification with Explainable AI using a Custom CNN: Comparative Analysis against Machine Learning Models
authors: Abir Mahmud Shahariar, Toma Saha, Sanjana Islam Kasfia, Taspia Salam
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence &amp; Networking (QPAIN)
abstract: The Phoenician alphabet is denoted as the ancestor of modern major writing systems, which remains a puzzle for contemporary acknowledgment due to its limited digital resources and complex symbols. This study proposes a custom CNN model which scored 95 % accuracy, surmounting renowned machine learning and transfer learning models involving Random Forest (78 %), Decision Tree (44 %), InceptionV3 (54 %), MobileNetV2 (86 %), and DenseNet121 (84 %) for Phoenician handwritten alphabet classification across 19 different classes using a custom dataset. To ensure transparency, we incorporate Explainable AI approaches, particularly LIME, Saliency Map and SHAP, on our proposed model, which demonstrate glyphs discriminative regions by disclosing understanding about feature relevance. Evaluation of performance through accuracy, recall, f1-score, confusion matrix, and AUC (1.00) validated the discriminative ability and reliability of the model. This study not only assists in the digitization and preservation of ancient scripts but also presents a framework for combining digital epigraphy with explainable deep learning.

title: Dynamic scene graph for Multi-stage Prediction and Planning in Autonomous Driving
authors: Nan Xu, Chaoyi Chen, Liang Chu, Minghe Na, Chong Guo, Yifan Jia
abstract: Motion prediction and motion planning are critical modules in autonomous driving, and they exhibit a high degree of correlation. Many prior works treat prediction and planning as separate modules, which may cause inter-module error accumulation and make it difficult to capture evolving future interactions between the ego vehicle and surrounding traffic agents, especially in long-horizon interactive scenarios. To address this issue, this paper proposes an integrated prediction-planning framework, DSGPlanner, which iteratively performs prediction and planning, and constructs a dynamic scene graph from prediction results to explicitly model future interactions between the ego vehicle and other agents. Unlike single-stage interaction methods, this framework updates the scene graph at each time step, allowing interaction information to be progressively refined as predictions evolve, thereby effectively mitigating the loss of interaction cues in long-horizon planning. Extensive experiments on the nuPlan benchmark demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method. Furthermore, although integration can reduce information loss, it may exacerbate causal confusion. To alleviate causal confusion in the integrated prediction-planning model and enhance computational efficiency, a dynamic mask self-distillation method is also introduced.

title: FreqMamba: Frequency Enhancement and Mamba-Driven Representation Learning for Pedestrian Attribute Recognition
authors: Junyi Wu, Yan Huang, Min Gao, Yuzhen Niu, Fusheng Li, Yuzhong Chen, Zhang Zhang, Qiang Wu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: This paper focuses on research into multimodal pedestrian attribute recognition (PAR). Existing multimodal PAR methods face three major challenges: (1) reliance on fixed prompt templates that limit refined textual attribute descriptions, (2) neglect of essential visual details, such as textures and contours, which are crucial for distinguishing subtle attributes, and (3) insufficient interaction between visual and textual modalities, resulting in suboptimal recognition performance. To address these challenges, we propose a Frequency Enhancement and Mamba-Driven Representation Learning (FreqMamba) framework. Specifically, we design a Dynamic Prompt Strategy (DPS) that dynamically adapts textual descriptions based on visual features, resulting in refined textual descriptions. Additionally, a Frequency-Enhanced Module (FEM) is introduced to capture texture and contour details in the frequency domain, enriching visual features and further enhancing the generation of dynamic prompts. Finally, we propose a Mamba-Driven Modality Interaction (MDMI) method combined with a Bidirectional Modality Contrastive Loss (BMCLoss) to facilitate effective interaction between modalities, integrating visual and textual information comprehensively. Extensive experiments on multiple PAR datasets, including PETA, PA100K, RAPv1, and RAPv2, demonstrate the superiority of our FreqMamba approach over existing methods.

title: Twin-Sight Rover: A Mobile Robot with Real-Time Object Detection Using Dual YOLOv11n Model on Resource-Constrained Edge Device
authors: Md. Maruf Haque Shah, Labib Marwan Hoque, Al Masrafi Shahria
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence &amp; Networking (QPAIN)
abstract: Real-time object detection is vital for robots that must see and act quickly. Two critical requirements emerge: efficient inference on resource-constrained hardware and robust accuracy in dynamic, cluttered environments. This paper presents a low-cost robotic platform deploying dual YOLOv11n models on Raspberry Pi, with comparative evaluation on Pi 3B+ and Pi 5 using OpenCV-based processing. We deploy two pruned YOLOv11n models running concurrently to maintain computational efficiency. To enhance detection coverage, one model is trained on the MS COCO dataset while the other uses PASCAL VOC. Inference outputs from both models are fused frame-by-frame using NonMaximum Suppression (NMS). This fusion strategy improves robustness: when one model fails, the other often compensates, yielding more stable detections under occlusion, motion blur, and variable lighting. The system pipeline, from image capture to visualization, is implemented using OpenCV with TensorFlow Lite inference for maximum efficiency. Experimental validation on Raspberry Pi 3B+ and Pi 5 demonstrates that the dualmodel approach achieves functional frame rates with low power consumption (3.55W and 7.8W respectively) and robust detection quality. This provides a practical solution for edge robotics without external servers or hardware accelerators.

title: Catching Hackers by Watching Watts: A Measurement Study of Power-Only Attack Detection on Consumer IoT Devices
authors: Yuanyuan Zhou, Hashim Zia, Jiahui Qin, Sandra Siby, Anna Maria Mandalari
abstract: Security analysis of consumer Internet of Things (IoT) devices is increasingly constrained by limited visibility into encrypted traffic and closed firmware, which motivates the exploration of observable and privacy preserving external signals. Prior work has shown that adversarial activity can induce measurable changes in device power usage, indicating that power based intrusion detection is feasible, yet its stability and reliability under realistic operating conditions remain unclear. We address this gap through a systematic measurement study of 33 commercial IoT devices, collecting per second power traces and complete background traffic logs during idle operation and realistic active use under Denial of Service (DoS) and Reconnaissance attacks. Our analysis shows that power usage provides clear separation between benign and adversarial behavior in stable idle states, while legitimate activity introduces masking that varies across devices. We identify 4 behavioral regimes that describe how activity suppresses or alters attack related increments and analyze power stability and background traffic characteristics to explain the observed differences in detection strength. Our findings provide the first systematic large-scale characterization of when power-based detection remains reliable and when it degrades, highlighting the physical and network conditions that govern its robustness in realistic deployments.

title: Learn to Generate Complementary March Tests
authors: Ruiqi Zhu, Houjun Wang, Chongtao Guo, Yindong Xiao, Zhijian Dai, Rui Wu, Wanyu Yang, Geoffrey Ye Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI) Systems
abstract: March tests require balancing fault coverage (FC) and test length in embedded memory testing. This brief proposes a novel reinforcement-learning (RL)-based framework that learns optimal March tests by exploiting undetected fault statistics. First, we analyze March tests using a scalable state-tuple-based fault analyzer. Then, we formulate March test generation as a problem to maximize expected FC (EFC) under budget limits. By modeling this as a Markov decision process (MDP), we leverage deep Q-learning to find the optimal operation sequences in March tests, with rewards derived from the proposed fault analyzer. Experimental results demonstrate that our framework generates complementary March tests achieving complete residual FC across a broader range of fault models than the existing methods, with generation efficiency significantly improved through fault analysis completing in milliseconds.

title: Based on Improved Ant Colony Algorithm Identification of Abnormal Nodes in Fiber Optic Communication Networks
authors: Hanyi Wang, Jushang Li, Ya'nan Zhang, Shicheng Zuo, Ziming Fang, Jialin Ren
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Integrated Intelligence and Communication Systems (ICIICS)
abstract: When abnormal nodes appear in the optical fiber communication network, it affects the network communication efficiency, so it is crucial to quickly detect these abnormal nodes. We proposed an improved ant colony algorithm for identifying abnormal nodes. By setting a limited domain for the input data, the computational load within the data path range is significantly reduced, allowing for quick calculation of the optimal path within the limited domain. In the experiment, different nodes in the fiber optic network were tested, and the average accuracy of the system was 97.9%. The improved ant colony algorithm is insensitive to the total number of samples in the process of identifying abnormal nodes, and can adapt well to applications with large amounts of test data. In the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0 0 0}$</tex> sample test, the detection time was less than 5 s, and the performance was significantly improved.

title: Fractal Feature Extraction and Evolution Law of Urban Morphology Based on GIS and Deep Learning
authors: Ke Ni, Qinhan Xu
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Integrated Intelligence and Communication Systems (ICIICS)
abstract: Urban morphology complexity and variability are central issues of the Urban systems research. This paper combines the multi-step convolutional neural network and the attention mechanism to automatically extract the urban morphology's fractal features. Combining time series analysis and interpretability methods, it makes a systematic discovery of the patterns of evolutionary urban morphology. A multi-scale convolutional neural network model is proposed for achieving high-precision estimation of box dimension and information dimension. Furthermore, a GRU network is used to model fractal dimension temporal evolution, and SHAP framework is used to quantitatively analyze the weight contribution of the important driving factors. The fractal dimension of the three large urban agglomerations illustrates a continuing growth and subsequently a saturation. Building density (SHAP=0. 324) and road network density (SHAP <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=0.287$</tex>) are the driving factors, followed by land use mixing. This study has provided a quantitative basis for the dynamic simulation of urban morphology and spatial governance.

title: SHDLF: An Efficient Segmentation-Based Hybrid Deep Learning Framework for Liver and Tumor Detection Using CT Images
authors: Zakir Hossen, Mahbub Alam, Hasibul Islam, Amran Hossain
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence &amp; Networking (QPAIN)
abstract: The liver is one of the most important organs in the human body. However, when liver disease occurs in the form of a tumor, traditional identification from liver images is timeconsuming and challenging for radiologists due to variations in tumor size and pattern. Early detection is crucial as it can help save human lives. Therefore, it is essential to develop an automatic system that can detect tumors without human intervention. To address these issues, we propose an efficient segmentation-based hybrid deep learning model framework called SHDLF for liver and tumor detection by utilizing CT images. The proposed model combines two segmentation models, ResNet50 and UNet70 for improving tumor detection performance. The ResNet50 model is used for feature extraction, while the UNet70 model is utilized to accurately segment the tumor(s) from the liver. The collected liver CT images are utilized to train the proposed SHDLF model. We compared the proposed model with five segmentation models, including UNet, ResNet50, SegNet, DeepLabV3+, and Attention UNet to ensure model performance. The experimental results show that the proposed SHDLF model achieved superior performance with a training accuracy of 99.38 %, a precision of 99.13 %, a Recall of 95.78 %, an F 1 score of 95.32 %, an IoU score of 91.84 %, and a Dice score of 97.30 %. So, this model can assist radiologists in making faster and more accurate decisions, leading to better treatment outcomes for patients.

title: X-TNet: A Reproducible and Explainable Framework for Automated Ultrasound-Based Liver Fibrosis Staging
authors: Arjon Golder, Md Adnan Hossain, Md Intishar Ahmed, Ahammad Hossen Eazaz, Rakibul Hasan Sezan
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence &amp; Networking (QPAIN)
abstract: The absence of benchmarks that could be reproduced has significantly slowed the development of automated liver fibrosis staging based on B-mode ultrasound. This paper presents the X-TNet framework that directly sets a new standard of reaching a new 97.6 percent accuracy on a large public dataset with errors in the classification that reflect clinical ambiguity. The framework's primary contributions however, extend beyond performance. A rigorous ablation study delivers a key negative result: spatial attention offers no significant benefit (McNemar's Test, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{p} \boldsymbol{=} \mathbf{1. 0}$</tex>), validating a lean architecture and challenging common design assumptions. Furthermore, a pioneering trustworthiness analysis reveals a critical disconnect between accuracy and reliability: the model is dangerously overconfident, proving that post-hoc calibration is a mandatory prerequisite for clinical safety. X-TNet delivers the field's first robust, publiclyaudited benchmark, setting a new standard for transparent and clinically-aware model development.

title: Transformer Architectures for Romanized Bangla Dialect Understanding in Resource-Constrained Conversational Systems
authors: Hasin Almas Sifat, Koushik Biswas Arko, Foysal Ahmed Neloy, Ashiqur Rahman Saron, Abdus Salam
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence &amp; Networking (QPAIN)
abstract: Multilingual societies deploy conversational systems to handle informal event-driven, and non-standard languages. Often, these conversational systems' user inputs will include Romanized and dialect-based user inputs. In the case of Bangla, many existing Natural Language Processing (NLP) tools have been developed for use with native scripts and therefore, present a challenge in dealing with real-world conversational data based on Romanized formats. This paper outlines our evaluation of several transformer-based models as a method to classify five different types of regional dialects of Romanized Bangla. The dataset we used, Vashantor, consists of several hundred locations that include users inputting Romanized Bangla against five different regional dialects. Each of the transformer models (multilingual, Bangla-specific, and compact) were run against a common experimental design. Evaluations were performed using agreementbased, error-sensitive, efficiency-aware metrics, and standard accuracy metrics. Our results demonstrate that multilingual and/or ‘script compatible’ models have greater success than models that have been specifically trained for Bangla with mBERT, yielding the best balance of predictive performance, robustness, and operational efficiency. In addition, training dynamics and confusion matrices substantiate the stability and generalization ability that multilingual pre-training has over Bangla-specific models. Therefore, we have concluded that in order to conduct NLP in Romanized Bangla, it is critical to select Transformer architecture(s) that have ‘script compatibility’ within the context of conversational applications with low resource requirements that generate real-world use cases.

title: Ensemble Learning with Shap-Based Interpretability for Neonatal Seizure Detection From Multi-Channel EEG
authors: Sagar Kundu, Apurbo Saikat Roy, Kalyan Kumar Halder
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence &amp; Networking (QPAIN)
abstract: Neonatal seizures, nowadays, are increasing rapidly, indicating underlying neurological problems. It needs accurate and early identification to avoid adverse developmental outcomes. This study introduces a machine-learning framework for neonatal seizure detection from multi-channel electroencephalography (EEG) using an ensemble of gradient-boosting classifiers with SHAP-based interpretability. The research uses a publicly available raw EEG dataset (Zenodo) which has EEG recordings from 79 newborns. The raw EEG dataset has been preprocessed and temporally aligned with expert annotations, downsampled from 256 Hz to 32 Hz, segmented into non-overlapping 8-second windows, filtered to remove baseline drift, and normalized prior to feature extraction. For each channel, 15 clinically motivated time-domain and frequency-domain features (including Hjorth parameters, bandpower, and spectral entropy) have been extracted. This dataset was class imbalanced, and this issue has been solved by using a random oversampling technique, which has been applied only to a training split. Performance is evaluated using a window-level stratified <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$70 / 30$</tex> train-test split. This study has established an ensemble framework by using three gradient-boosting classifiers. SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) have been used to identify the most influential features that contribute to the prediction of seizures, supporting interpretability and clinical insight. The ensemble framework with 96 % accuracy, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{9 5. 5 \%}$</tex> precision and F1-score, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{9 5. 6 \%}$</tex> recall, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{9 7. 4 \%}$</tex> ROC-AUC, 88.7 % PR-AUC, 93.6 % seizure sensitivity, and 88.7 % specificity makes the system reliable and clinically insightful.

title: Adaptive Gradient-Weighted Stacking Ensemble for Electric Vehicle Energy Efficiency Prediction: A Novel Meta-Learning Approach
authors: Ashwin Jacob, H Mohammed, Gokul Krishnan, K. Saranya, V.Usha Rani, Parameswaran R
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence &amp; Networking (QPAIN)
abstract: One of the most important and complex issues of sustainable transportation is the energy efficiency prediction of electric vehicles (EVs), which is associated with intricate interdependences between the vehicles characteristics, motor capacity, charging ability, and performance efficiency. Conventional methods of machine learning do not always explain the complex non-linear dynamics and multi-scale relationships of EV energy consumption. This study presents a new meta-learning algorithm called Adaptive Gradient-Weighted Stacking (AGWS) Ensemble that combines both the gradient-based optimization and adaptive weight learning to achieve better energy efficiency prediction. The suggested system works with detailed EV data covering model year, manufacturer, vehicle type, motor power (kW), recharge time (hours) as well as car energy efficiency <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\text{km} / \text{kWh})$</tex>. AGWS has three levels of architecture (1) the heterogeneous base learners that render various facets of the efficiency landscape, (2) the adaptive optimization of weights depending on the prediction confidence, and the error pattern, and (3) Ridge regression meta-learning to combine the ensembles effectively. The comprehensive comparison of the results of AGWS with seven state-of-the-art algorithms indicates that it has an outstanding predictive accuracy with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{R} 2=0.9153, \text{RMSE}=0.2456, \text{MAE}{=}$</tex> 0.1694, and MAPE <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=4.0055 \%$</tex> indicating a 0.21 % improvement over the best model. The algorithm has identified motor power <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\text{kW})$</tex> as the most significant predictor having 35 percent feature importance, then vehicle make, and model characteristics. A consistent analysis of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{1 0}$</tex> visualization frameworks shows that AGWS has a consistent prediction accuracy in all of the vehicular classes and efficiency ranges, with high consistency in the region of mid-range efficiency vehicles <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(4-5 ~\text{km} / \text{kWh})$</tex>. The real-world EV selection, fleet optimization and policy-building in the sustainable transportation ecosystem are effectively deployed with the foundation model architecture.

title: Transformer-Based Natural Language Processing for Automated Bug Report Classification in Software Repositories
authors: Pothuraju Likitha, Kommu. Prasanthi, K.Naga Rajeswari
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence &amp; Networking (QPAIN)
abstract: The article explores transformer-based natural language processing to further the development of automated bug report classification in software repositories, overcoming the shortcomings of previous studies which mostly utilized a combination of a static text classification model or task-specific pre-trained transformers. Although previous research concentrated on coarse-grained categorization, vulnerability detection, deep learning project bug identification, or code repair, the presented work presents a dual-phase framework in which both contextual and adaptive and temporal aspects of bug management are combined. It presents a transformer-based model, which involves inputs of multi-source such as bug titles, description, reproduction-steps, and metadata that are integrated together by an adaptive fusion of attention mechanism. The research shows that this richer representation is more effective in increasing classification accuracy than traditional transformer fine-tuning methods by retrieving linguistic and structural hints in reports. It is based on this that will elaborate on the way automated classification systems can be developed over time by learning repository-aware. The analysis of temporal drift is used to track changes in the distribution of bugs, whereas the developer-in-the-loop feedback ensures never-ending improvement. Self-corrective attention mechanism is also used to dynamically re-weight contextual features by prediction confidence and historic classification patterns. According to the research, this changing framework does not only increase the long-term resilience and flexibility but also builds a scalable paradigm of intelligent and context-sensitive bug triaging in contemporary software engineering ecosystems.

title: IoT-Based Electricity Consumption Prediction with User-Controlled Appliance Shutdown
authors: Haripriya V, Fathima Rania M, Raghavi S
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence &amp; Networking (QPAIN)
abstract: The energy consumption in households has been continuously growing as a result of the use of more electrical and electronic appliances. Traditional smart meters cannot anticipate the future and control the work of appliances and provide only basic indicators of their use. To enhance the state of domestic electricity consumption, the proposed study introduces an Internet of Things-based energy management system entailing real-time monitoring, predictive modelling, and automated cost estimation. Smart switches and sensors track the real time energy consumption of every appliance, and the data are processed by machine learning models to predict demand trends in the short- run. The system allows its users to remotely switch off appliances to reduce unnecessary use of energy and sends notifications when the expected amount of consumption exceeds the set limits. It also gives households a clear picture of their spending in relation to electricity by approximating the rates of electricity cost. As per the experimental analysis, an integration of IoT equipment with predictive forecasting brings about improved awareness of energy consumption, reduction of waste, and ease of making well- informed decisions in the day-to-day management of energy consumption at the household level.

title: EOCNet: Elongated Oriented Convolution for High-Aspect-Ratio Object Detection in SAR Imagery
authors: Peixuan Li, Ziqi Ye, Haipeng Wang
abstract: Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) object detection is hindered by intense speckle noise and targets with extreme aspect ratios. Traditional square kernels struggle to represent elongated structures and often capture redundant background clutter. We propose the Elongated Oriented Convolution Network (EOCNet), a dual-domain framework designed for robust SAR detection. In the frequency domain, Multi-level Wavelet Convolution (WTConv) achieves exponential receptive field expansion while suppressing high-frequency noise. In the spatial domain, Elongated Oriented Convolution (EOC) extracts directional features along primary axes to enhance narrow-structure representation. Furthermore, an optimized localization head embedded with EOC modules ensures precise boundary alignment. Experiments on MSAR-1.0, FAIR-CSAR, and SAR-Airport-1.0 datasets demonstrate that EOCNet outperforms state-of-the-art methods such as Oriented R-CNN and ReDet, achieving mAPs of 88.8%, 52.0%, and 99.6%, respectively.

title: Restoration-Guided Multi-Hazard Detection in Hazy Remote Sensing Images
authors: Ran Tian, Lijian Yu, Xiangchao Meng, Haoyong Qi
abstract: Geological hazard segmentation in remote sensing images is challenging due to the large variation in morphology and scale among different hazard types, as well as the severe degradation caused by haze. In particular, large region-like hazards such as landslides and collapses require strong contextual modeling, whereas crack-like hazards depend more on fine structural preservation. To address these issues, we propose a restoration-guided framework for multi-hazard segmentation in hazy remote sensing images. The proposed network integrates an auxiliary dehazing branch with a coarse-to-fine segmentation head, enabling joint learning of degradation-robust representations and structural features for hazard extraction. Specifically, restoration-aware features are fused with encoder features to enhance segmentation under degraded conditions, while the coarse and fine branches are used to model large-scale hazard regions and slender local structures, respectively. Experiments on a self-constructed hazy multi-hazard dataset containing multiple geological hazard categories and degraded haze scenarios demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: 1.2 kV-Class All Ion-Implanted Vertical GaN-DMOSFETs with Superior Short-Circuit Ruggedness
authors: Nao Suganuma, Katsunori Ueno, Ryo Tanaka, Hirohisa Hirai, Akira Nakajima, Shinsuke Harada, Shinya Takashima
publish: 2026 IEEE 38th International Symposium on Power Semiconductor Devices and ICs (ISPSD)
abstract: The 1.2 kV-class vertical GaN planar-gate MOSFETs were fabricated using ion implantation to control structural parameters, and their short-circuit withstand capability was evaluated. The device with a narrower JFET width exhibited superior short-circuit ruggedness, achieving a short-circuit withstand time of 29.9 μs under conditions of 175°C and a V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">bus</inf> of 800 V. This improvement is attributed to the reduction of short-circuit current due to current limitation in the JFET region and the positive temperature coefficient of threshold voltage provided by the c-plane GaN-MOS interface characteristics. Furthermore, the device demonstrated an avalanche voltage of 1750 V and an excellent avalanche energy capability of 64.7 mJ/cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> in Unclamped Inductive Switching (UIS) tests. These findings represent a significant step toward the practical realization of vertical GaN power devices.

title: A Novel Trench IGBT with Floating P-Region Controlled Split Gate for Low EMI Noise
authors: Tongyang Wang, Zehong Li, Xuan Wang, Ziming Xia, Yige Zheng, Zhaoqing Jin, Min Ren
publish: 2026 IEEE 38th International Symposium on Power Semiconductor Devices and ICs (ISPSD)
abstract: A novel trench insulated gate bipolar transistor (IGBT) with floating P-Region controlled split gate (FPCSG) is proposed. A parasitic MOS is introduced in the split gate (SG) and is controlled by the electrostatic potential of the floating P-Region (V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">FP</inf>). During turn-off, the SG is depleted, increasing its electrostatic potential (V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">SG</inf>). During turn-on, once V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">FP</inf> reaches the threshold voltage of the parasitic MOS, V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">SG</inf> rapidly drops to 0 V. This generates a large displacement current that discharges the FP, thereby reducing both dV<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">FP</inf>/dt and I<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">G_dis</inf>. TCAD simulation results show that, compared to the hole path IGBT (HP-IGBT), the FPCSG-IGBT achieves an 86.0% lower dV<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">FP</inf>/dt, an 84.9% lower dI<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">C</inf>/dt<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">max</inf> at the same R<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">G</inf>, and an 89.0% lower dV<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">KA</inf>/dt<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">max</inf> at the same E<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">on</inf>, significantly reducing electromagnetic interference (EMI) noise without degradation of other electrical parameters.

title: DeTailNet: Scene Graph Generation with Selective Causal Debiasing and Generative Tail Enhancement
authors: Yin YeChen, Zhong Zhenshu, Sun Qian
abstract: Scene graph generation aims to jointly model object entities and their semantic relationships in images. However, existing methods are often affected by the joint influence of long-tailed data distribution and contextual statistical bias during relation prediction, which leads to an over-reliance on high-frequency relation priors and weak recognition of tail predicates, thereby limiting the completeness and accuracy of scene semantic representation. To address these issues, this paper proposes a scene graph generation method that integrates selective total direct effect debiasing with generative tail enhancement. In the relation classification stage, a selective total direct effect debiasing mechanism is introduced to adaptively regulate the intervention strength on biased predictions, suppressing erroneous relation inference caused by object co-occurrence statistics and language priors while preserving discriminative visual semantic cues. In the tail predicate modeling stage, a generative adversarial learning based tail enhancement module is designed to compensate and enrich low-frequency relation features, alleviating insufficient feature learning caused by tail-sample scarcity. Experimental results on the PredCls task of the Visual Genome dataset show that the proposed method effectively improves tail predicate recognition while maintaining competitive overall recall, demonstrating its effectiveness in suppressing contextual bias and mitigating long-tail effects. Although validated on a general natural-image benchmark, the proposed framework also shows promising transfer potential to remote sensing imagery and spaceborne Earth observation scenarios, providing a useful reference for semantic scene understanding, object relation reasoning, and intelligent image interpretation in aerospace information processing.

title: Tuberculosis Detection from Chest X-Rays: A Transformer-Based Deep Learning Approach for Faster and More Accurate Diagnosis
authors: Tanjid Hasan Turky, Fernaz Narin Nur
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence &amp; Networking (QPAIN)
abstract: Tuberculosis (TB) is a serious problem worldwide, especially in low and middle-income countries where access to expert radiologists is low. Chest X-ray (CXR) screening is highly popular but manual interpretation is a time-consuming task and is subject to inter-observer variability. This study investigates transformer-based architectures for automated tuberculosis (TB) detection from chest X-ray images. A Swin Transformers model was evaluated with stratified 5-fold cross-validation on a publicly available dataset with 4200 images (700 TB and 3500 normal). To overcome class imbalance, cost-sensitive learning was applied with the use of class-weighted loss functions. On an internal test set held-out of 840 images (700 normal, 140 TB), the Swin Transformer obtained 99.29% accuracy, 0.96 TB sensitivity, 1.00 specificity and a TB F1-score of 0.98. Five fold cross validated performance was stable with narrow confidence interval. Out-of-domain testing on a separate data set of 3,008 images gave 85.17% accuracy, 0.8821 sensitivity, 0.7043 specificity which shows the effects of domain shift. Grad-CAM was carried out to give visual explanations and validate clinically relevant attention. Results suggest the proposed framework is an excellent candidate as a decision support tool for mass screening for TB, provided it is validated in additional multi-center studies and adaptation of the framework to additional domains is conducted.

title: Exploring AI-Assisted Translation and Climate Data Extraction from Alaskan Russian Missionary Diaries: Arctic Climate Research
authors: Abigail Galarza, Valeria Quintero, Christoph Lauter, Olga Lauter, Alex Mayer, Benjamin Good
publish: 2026 IEEE Southwest Symposium on Image Analysis and Interpretation (SSIAI)
abstract: Climate change is affecting Arctic regions at an alarming rate. To understand climate change impacts and adaptations in Alaska, more data is needed on historical climate phenomena. The travel journals from Russian missionaries of the Lower Yukon Region in Alaska from the 19th to early 20th centuries are a potential source of historical environmental knowledge. These historic travel journals—written in pre-reform Russian orthography—are largely inaccessible to both modern communities and researchers. Here, our interdisciplinary team assesses the ability of a large language model (LLM), Chat GPT, to translate these travel journals and extract climatic data. This pilot project aims to identify temporal and environmental patterns within the journals through extraction of dates and weatherrelated terms. We manually verified date and weatherword occurrences, extracted with Chat GPT, from scans of approximately 400 journal entries spanning four years to generate training data for LLM supervised learning. Results show strong seasonality, with diary activity peaking in spring (approximately 78% above the mean) and declining in late summer and fall (approximately 60% below the mean). The frequency patterns of the weather-words indicate the environmental conditions that influenced travel and operations were more likely to be recorded. This groundwork lays the foundation for future LLM supervised learning.

title: An Intelligent AI Framework for Early Parkinson's Disease Diagnosis Using PCA-Enhanced Vocal Feature Engineering
authors: Mohammad Tahbib Ullah, Shanjida Hossain Shefa, Sk Tahmed Salim Rafid, Amit Saha, Md Aslamuzzaman Bappy, Md. Alif Sheakh
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence &amp; Networking (QPAIN)
abstract: Parkinson's disease is a neurological condition that primarily impairs movement and motor function and significantly drops a patient's quality of life. Detecting PD early is necessary for effective disease management and timely medical intervention. In this research, we propose a machine learning system using vocal features as a non-invasive and accessible biomarker. Our study begins with exploratory data analysis, followed by feature selection methods to find the most impactful attributes. Then, we utilized several machine learning algorithms, including Decision Tree, Naïve Bayes, Support Vector Classifier, Random Forest, Logistic Regression,XGBoost, K-Nearest Neighbor, and AdaBoost, and calculated predictive performance using relevant metrics. To enhance our model performance, a hyperparameter tuning strategy is implemented to optimize the learning period. The XGBoost classifier, optimized through the principal component analysis feature selection technique and subsequent hyperparameter tuning, demonstrated robust performance. This work attained the highest accuracy of 97.44 % and shows that this fine-tuned model is both reliable and well-suited for the detection.

title: PCA-Enhanced Logistic Regression Model Framework for Intelligent EV Battery Charging Duration Classification with Comparative Machine Learning Analysis
authors: Ashwin Jacob, Sibi Kumaran K, Saran Adithyaa S, Thamizharasan R, S. Nalini Jayanthi, T. Rajesh
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence &amp; Networking (QPAIN)
abstract: The fast rate of electric vehicle (EV) integration requires the use of smart battery control systems that are able to predict optimal charge times to ensure the battery life, grid stability, and energy consumption. Conventional methods of machine learning do not typically handle the non-linear and complicated nature of the relationship between charging parameters (SOC, voltage, current, temperature, degradation rates) and optimal charging categories. Largescale experimental analysis of real-world EV battery charging data reveals that the PCA (Principal Component Analysis) enhanced logistic regression provides much better results (97.5% accuracy, Precision: 0.975, Recall: 0.975, F1-Score: 0.975), but GMM provides similar performance (93.5% accuracy, Precision: 0.935, Recall: 0.935, F1-Score: 0.935) with the benefit of having prob The effectiveness of the probabilistic modeling approaches is attested by the comparative study against the Support Vector Machine (92.0% accuracy), Gaussian Naive Bayes (87.0% accuracy) and K-Nearest Neighbors (83.5% accuracy). The framework gives quantification of uncertainty in the predictions, which is extremely good in the real-time charging station management systems where the confidence intervals are crucial. Our contribution makes computational battery analytics advance with an effective and explanatory framework of optimal classification of EV charging duration that is probabilistically based.

title: Analytical Solution for Crosswell Electromagnetic Detection with Cased Boreholes
authors: Kai Zhao, Decheng Hong
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: We propose an analytical algorithm for accurately solving the electromagnetic (EM) fields within a configuration of two parallel, multilayered cylindrical structures. In this model, a triaxial dipole transmitter is located in the innermost layer of the first cylinder, while EM multi-component measurements are recorded within the innermost layer of the second cylinder. The field is represented as a combination of outgoing waves and standing waves, with the use of the generalized reflection/transmission (R/T) method to characterize wave propagation. The core of the algorithm lies in the transformation between these wave types, achieved through the application of Graf's addition theorems across dual cylindrical coordinate systems. This approach enables the derivation of a super reflection coefficient on a fictitious boundary, specifically addressing eccentricity. By integrating this with the conventional generalized R/T method, the exact field at any spatial point can be determined. The derived formulae have been rigorously validated through numerical experiments and are applicable to crosswell EM investigations involving either single or double-cased wells.

title: Understanding Learner Perceptions in Distance Education Using Sentiment Classification
authors: Md. Saiful Islam, Siyam Ul Alam, Syeda Zakia Nayem, Saurav Chandra Das, Md. Morshed Ali
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence &amp; Networking (QPAIN)
abstract: Distance learning has become a crucial method of education, especially in the period of the COVID-19 pandemic, which also allows learning and teaching to take place outside regular classrooms in both synchronous and asynchronous environments. It is fundamental to ensure an improvement in the effectiveness of this modality by gaining an understanding of the perception of this modality among the population. The work is an analysis using Natural Language Processing (NLP) and machine learning models on a Kaggle dataset of user-created comments expressing their sentiments about distance learning. Sentiment labeling of the comments was done using VADER (Valence Aware Dictionary and sEntiment Reasoner), which groups comments into positive, negative, and neutral. The positive sentiments related to words such as “accessibility” and “flexibility,” whereas words that indicate challenging issues of technology and infrastructure included negative sentiments. A variety of models were tried to compare the sentiment classification, including SVM, logistic regression, CNN, LSTM, BiLSTM, random forest, and BERT. Out of them, BERT established the best accuracy (95%), followed by BiLSTM (94%; and SVM/LSTM (93%). The results reveal that although distance learning is mostly valued because of it's flexibility and it's ability to be inclusive, there is some concern around the limitations of technicality and quality of learning experiences. These observations suggest a good implication for educators, institutions, and policymakers to improve the digital learning systems. In the future, aspect-based sentiment analysis and real-time feedback can be incorporated to enhance the learner.

title: Optimization of Fault Diagnosis Algorithm and Real Time Monitoring System Based on Electronic Information Equipment
authors: Fengqi Jia, Shanshan Lin, Xiaoli Shan
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Integrated Intelligence and Communication Systems (ICIICS)
abstract: Background: Traditional electronic fault diagnosis relies on manual feature extraction, is insensitive to early weak faults, and lacks real-time monitoring. This paper optimizes fault diagnosis algorithms and constructs a real-time monitoring system. Methods: A 1D-CNN adaptively extracts multi-level fault features from vibration signals, with residual connections improving model stability and convergence. Genetic algorithms optimize deep neural network parameters to enhance generalization and accuracy. An adaptive threshold strategy reduces false positives, while online incremental learning captures new fault modes without catastrophic forgetting. An edge-cloud collaborative architecture enables local fast inference and cloud-based global management. Results: The method achieves 95.12% diagnostic accuracy and 93.72% recall under six fault scenarios, with a 1.21% false alarm rate and 15 ms response delay. Conclusion: This study integrates deep learning and optimization algorithms to improve fault diagnosis accuracy, real-time performance, and long-term reliability for predictive maintenance.

title: Adaptive Learning in Non-Stationary Computer Science Environments with Self-Evolving AI Systems
authors: C. Maddilety, Golla Lingesh, Kanchamreddy Siva Sankar Reddy, Kapulapalli Sai Pradeep Reddy, Gaddam Charan Teja
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Integrated Intelligence and Communication Systems (ICIICS)
abstract: The uses of Modern Computer Science and Engineering (CSE) such as cybersecurity monitoring, IoT analytics, software log analysis are highly non-stationary and their changing data distributions introduce concept drift and rapidly worsen the performance of fixed machine learning models. This paper proposes a solution to such a challenge in the form of Self-Evolving Artificial Intelligence (SEAI) framework, where concept drift detect, meta-learning, continual learning, self-supervising representation learning, and adaptive transformer architecture are combined into one online framework. SEAI recognizes changes in distributional changes automatically and builds its model fast through meta-learning initializing and overcomes the problem of catastrophic forgetting through continual learning regularization and constrained replay. The framework is also tested with the help of CICIDs-2021 data on cybersecurity, the IoT-23 data on streaming traffic and altering software logs data. The experiment findings indicate that the drift-detecting precision is 91.4, adaptation latency is 0.7 seconds, and forgetting is much lower than in the scenario of stable transformers and replaybased continual learning baselines, without disastrously high memory consumption. The findings substantiate the fact that SEAI is useful in offering robust and reliable adaptive intelligence on non-stationary real world CSE environments.

title: Comparative Analysis under Laboratory Conditions of the Neutral Grounding Techniques in Electrical Networks
authors: Daniela-Florentina Roșca, Ana-Bianca Mititelu, Pavel Atănăsoae
publish: 2026 International Conference on Development and Application Systems (DAS)
abstract: Neutral point treatment is a fundamental aspect of the design and operation of electrical power generation, transmission, distribution, and consumption networks. This paper presents an analysis of neutral point treatment techniques used in medium-voltage electrical distribution networks, with a focus on system behavior in the event of single-phase faults. The study comparatively analyzes, under laboratory conditions, two competing neutral treatment methods that result in increased currents and voltages, respectively, in the event of a single-phase fault: the isolated neutral and the grounded neutral. To investigate the system’s behavior, a simulation model of a medium-voltage distribution network was developed in the MATLAB Simulink environment. The phenomena that occur during short circuits and ground faults are highlighted and validated using the laboratory test bench.

title: An Explainable Deep Learning Framework for Multi-Class Alzheimer's Diagnosis With Multi-Scale CNN
authors: Md. Ashraful Hossen Akash, Shyla Afroge, Md. Asif Sajeed, Md Barkullah, Abul Khayer
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence &amp; Networking (QPAIN)
abstract: Early detection and accurate staging of Alzheimer's disease (AD) from structural MRI scans are crucial for timely clinical intervention. However, many existing deep learning approaches rely on conventional CNN architectures, are sensitive to evaluation design choices, and provide limited interpretability or deployment considerations. To address these challenges, this work presents a multi-scale, custom-designed Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) integrated with a MobileNetV2-based preprocessing stem and progressive convolutional blocks to capture structural MRI features at multiple receptive fields. Spatial feature consolidation with reduced computational overhead is achieved through a dual-path pooling mechanism and a Global Average Pooling-based classification head. An ensemble combining the proposed CNN with Xception and ResNet50 by majority vote is introduced to enhance prediction robustness for four AD stages: Non-Demented, Very Mild Demented, Mild Demented, and Moderate Demented. Strong multiclass performance was achieved when experiments were conducted on the Alzheimer's Disease Augmented MRI dataset, achieving an average accuracy of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{9 9. 1 5 \%} \pm \mathbf{0. 0 4}$</tex> and an F1-score of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{0. 9 9 1 5} \pm \mathbf{0. 0 0 0 4}$</tex> under a 3fold stratified cross-validation protocol, indicating high predictive performance with low variance across folds. So as to improve model transparency, discriminative neuroanatomical regions associated with disease severity have been visualized through the use of Grad-CAM-guided backpropagation, Integrated Gradients, and LIME. Moreover, the proposed framework has been deployed as a lightweight web application and made publicly available to showcase the real-time inference capability. The results indicate that the proposed approach offers an accurate and interpretable framework for multi-class AD staging and conveys promising potential for future clinical screening research.

title: Paving the Way for 6G – Radio Enhancements for Extended Reality in 5G-Advanced Release-19
authors: Stefano Paris, Klaus I. Pedersen, Ebin Chacko, Abolfazl Amiri, Claudio Rosa, Santiago Morejon, Zexian Li, Umar Saeed, Boyan Yanakiev
abstract: This article describes the latest advancements for real-time eXtended Reality (XR) communications over 3GPP 5G-Advanced Release-19, as well as the anticipated enhancements for the upcoming 6G radio design. Release-19 introduces two main categories of enhancements: those for better supporting delay-criticalXRtraffic, such as measurement gap skipping and uplink scheduling improvements like latency aware logical channel prioritization and enhancements to delay status reporting, which significantly increase system-levelXRcapacity; and application-awareness enablers, focusing on uplink rate control for congestion management via application rate adaptation and multi-modality enhancements. Looking ahead the 6G radio design is projected to further elevate XR performance through advanced physical layer and RAN protocol enhancements. System-level simulation results for 3.5GHz with 100MHz bandwidth demonstrate promising capacity increases compared to 5G, and the performance is expected to be further enhanced by utilizing new mid-bands, such as around 7GHz, with extreme MIMO and wider bandwidths like 200MHz.
