This study presents a lightweight ensemble framework that integrates handcrafted textual features with layered fusion strategies. Structural, stylistic, semantic, and readability-based features-such as punctuation frequency, capitalization ratio, and title-body similarity-were extracted and represented numerically. These features were used to train diverse classifiers, including Logistic Regression, Random Forest, Extra Trees, XGBoost, and LightGBM. A two-level ensemble was designed: first, base models were combined through soft voting; second, their outputs were stacked using Logistic Regression as a meta-learner. Experiments on the “Fake and Real News” benchmark dataset achieved 91% accuracy and an AUC of 0.968, surpassing the performance of individual classifiers and deep learning baselines. The proposed approach offers a practical and interpretable solution for realtime fake news detection where transparency and computational efficiency are essential.

title: Advancing Dental Care with Deep Learning: Classification and Detection of Dental Diseases in OPG X-Ray Images
authors: Sabrina Akter Esha, Miftahul Jannat, Md. Jahidul Hasan Jahid, Anika Bushra
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence &amp; Networking (QPAIN)
abstract: Using Orthopantomogram (OPG) X-ray images, which are frequently utilized in clinical dentistry to offer a thorough view of the jaws, teeth, and surrounding structures, our work intends to construct a deep learning-based dental disease diagnosis system. Deep learning approaches have been used by several studies to diagnose dental diseases such as cavities, infections, impacted teeth, fractures, and implants using OPGs. These studies have shown that OPG images can greatly improve diagnostic accuracy and help doctors with early identification and treatment planning when combined with automated detection systems. We merged two datasets, one from Kaggle and another from AIUB, into a unified dataset that covers a wide range of dental abnormalities. After applying image enhancement techniques and class-wise cropping, multiple transfer learning and object detection models were trained and tested. The best performance was achieved using a fine-tuned Inception-based model, which reached an accuracy of 93.7% ± 0.6% (95% CI: 92.6% – 94.8%) accuracy, and a hybrid model combining Inception and EfficientNet, which also delivered the same accuracy as InceptionV3. Furthermore, Object detection was explored by converting the dataset annotation to YOLO format and implementing different YOLO models. Performance across all approaches was evaluated using standard metrics and statistical analysis, confirming the effectiveness and robustness of deep learning for dental disease detection from OPG images.

title: Low-altitude Electromagnetic Spectrum Sensing Acceleration Method based on FFT and Parallel Pipeline
authors: Hongcan Huang, Zhenjia Chen, Hongpeng Chen, Mengqiang Li, WanchaoLi WanchaoLi
abstract: Aiming at the real-time and efficiency requirements of spectrum sensing in low-altitude information monitoring scenes, a low-altitude electromagnetic spectrum sensing acceleration method based on fast Fourier transform and parallel pipeline was designed and implemented. In this method, the self-made RF front-end and ADC module are used to complete the digital processing, and the data are directly input to the FPGA for stream calculation. On the FPGA side, the parallel pipeline structure is used to realize the FFT, threshold decision and other key algorithms. As the main control platform, RK3568 is responsible for system control and result processing, and an efficient hardware/software cooperation architecture is constructed. Experimental results show that the proposed method performs well in terms of real-time performance, energy efficiency and scalability, and provides an efficient and low delay implementation scheme for low-altitude electromagnetic spectrum sensing.

title: Design and Analysis of a Cell Phone Mounted Dual-Band Patch Antenna for 5G/6G Applications
authors: Md Faruk Muntasir Banna, Mehedi Hasan Munna, Md Aminul Islam, Syed Md Hasin Tasnim
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence &amp; Networking (QPAIN)
abstract: In this paper, we proposed a design and analysis of a dual-band microstrip patch antenna aimed at next-gen Wi-Fi and 5G/6G mobile applications. Nowadays, microstrip patch antennas are commonly used in wireless systems because they are low profile, easy to fabricate, and compatible with printed circuit boards. The proposed antenna is an inset-fed dual band rectangular patch antenna. It was designed on an FR4 substrate which operates at 5.2 GHz and 6.96 GHz with return losses of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$-24 \mathbf{~ d B}$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$-45 \mathbf{~ d B}$</tex>, respectively. To verify its practical usability, the antenna was placed on the back of a smartphone model, and a full-wave simulation was done using CST Studio. The results indicate that the antenna maintains stable operation in both bands. It has acceptable gain and radiation characteristics, even in the presence of the phone body. Furthermore, a Specific Absorption Rate (SAR) analysis was done in CST using a multilayer human body phantom model. The obtained SAR values were found to be well below internationally accepted safety limits. So, it is safe to use with any smartphone models. Overall, the proposed antenna has a small and practical design, making it simple to manufacture and easy to integrate into modern smartphones. It is well suited for research purposes and supports Wi-Fi as well as emerging sub- <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$6 \text{GHz} 5 \mathrm{G} / 6 \mathrm{G}$</tex> wireless technologies.

title: Not All Encoder Layers Are Essential: Shallow-Feature Fusion for Infrared Small Target Detection
authors: Qiang Li, Jiangbin Zheng, Wenbin Zou, Yong Zhao, Bingshu Wang, Han Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Deep learning has revolutionized infrared small target detection. However, prevailing encoder-decoder architectures, which maintain symmetrical depths, incur unnecessary computational cost. We challenge this design with a key insight: for this task, deep encoder layers are largely dispensable in the decoder feature fusion process, as shallow features alone can achieve an optimal accuracy-efficiency balance. Driven by this insight, we propose TADNet, a Target-Aware Dual-branch Network, to translate this insight into an efficient and accurate model. Its core components incorporate: (1) an effective dual-branch encoder that collaboratively extracts high-resolution spatial details and rich semantic contexts, and (2) an efficient dynamic fusion decoder that strategically utilizes only the first two shallowest features— pruning the traditional fusion backbone. Beyond the model, we introduce the NPU-SIRST benchmark to address multi-scale and multi-scenario limitations. Rigorous experiments on four datasets show that our method achieves state-of-the-art results with significantly enhanced efficiency, demonstrating robust transferability under infrared cross-dataset testing. The code is available at: <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/searchfresh/TADNet</uri>.

title: AI Driven CNN-LSTM Hybrid Neural Network for Robust Heart Disease Prediction
authors: Md Abdulla Hasan, Zaffar Abdullah, Faiza Saiara Nity
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence &amp; Networking (QPAIN)
abstract: Heart disease is one of the major causes of death worldwide and predictive models that are applicable within a wide range of patients are needed; however, existing machine learning and deep learning approaches rely on shallow network structures or single-source datasets and lack robustness in heterogeneous clinical settings. This paper presents a Hybrid Deep Neural Network (HDNN) which combines convolutional neural networks (CNNs) with hierarchical interaction of feature with long short-term memory (LSTM) networks with gated dependencies together with a relevance-sensitive weighting scheme and risk-sensitive loss to achieve enhanced optimization stability and clinical sensitivity. The framework is evaluated based on the dual-scale validation framework that includes the UCI Cleveland benchmark dataset and a multi-source dataset constructed by integrating of five publicly available heart disease repositories. The proposed HDNN evaluates at 97.75 % (AUC <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=0.9885$</tex>) accuracy on Cleveland and 98.86 % (AUC = 0.9978) on the multi-source dataset and, overall, it demonstrates superior performance over the baseline machine and deep learning models with clinically relevant indicators. These results demonstrate that the suggested framework provides a robust and reliable generalizable method of heart disease risk prediction with promising implications for clinical decision support.

title: Multimodal Domain Generalization for Inter-Turn Short-Circuit Fault Diagnosis of Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motors
authors: He Bing, Shi Jin, Guanglong Jia, Ming Jing, Fuqi Li
abstract: The current characteristics of inter-turn short-circuit (ITSC) faults in permanent magnet synchronous motor (PMSM) typically exhibit continuous evolution characteristics with changes in operating conditions. However, industrial data acquisition usually only covers limited and discrete operating conditions, making it difficult to fully characterize fault evolution information across operating conditions. To address this issue, this article proposes a multimodal domain generalization diagnostic framework. The proposed framework constructs feature representations from three physical perspectives, namely the time domain, frequency domain, and time–frequency domain, based on the same stator current signal. By introducing an operating-condition evolution prior, the proposed framework extends conventional modeling approaches that treat operating conditions as discrete domains to a continuous evolution representation, thereby recovering the progressive trajectories of fault features in the latent space. In addition, semantic-guided multimodal feature fusion and a hierarchical domain alignment mechanism are jointly employed to mitigate modality heterogeneity and cross-condition distribution shifts. Experimental results on both private and public datasets demonstrate that the proposed method maintains stable and accurate diagnostic performance under unseen operating conditions, validating its effectiveness for cross-condition ITSC fault diagnosis in PMSM.

title: Towards Cross-Satellite Change Detection: A Spatial-Spectral Dual-Alignment Joint Learning Network
authors: Kai Zhang, Jian Xiao, Feng Zhang, Jiande Sun, Lorenzo Bruzzone
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Despite boosting change detection performance, most deep neural networks (DNNs) are designed for multi-temporal remote sensing images acquired by the same satellite. However, it is difficult for a single satellite to acquire continuous data for high-frequency change detection over a large area. Although it is feasible to conduct CD on multi-temporal remote sensing images from different satellites, differences in spectral response and spatial resolution cannot be ignored. To mitigate these issues, we propose a spatial-spectral dual-alignment joint learning network (SSDA-Net). The proposed framework introduces a dual-resolution joint learning strategy that exploits LR features from the SR network to guide HR feature alignment in the CD network, thereby suppressing spectral inconsistencies caused by different sensor responses and spatial mismatches induced by resolution differences between satellites. Specifically, for multi-temporal inputs with different resolutions, we first send the LR one into the SR network to improve its spatial resolution, and then the super-resolved result and the HR one are fed into the CD network to extract HR features. To exploit the spatial and spectral information in both LR and HR features, we design dual-alignment learning modules (DALMs), which are composed of a spectral alignment block and a spatial alignment block. Through the two modules, the spectral and spatial differences between multi-temporal images from different satellites are suppressed. Finally, features from DALMs are used to generate the change map. To validate the effectiveness of the proposed SSDA-Net, we construct a real cross-satellite change detection (SD-CSCD) dataset. Experimental results on the newly-built SD-CSCD dataset, the DE-CD dataset, and a synthesized dataset demonstrate that our SSDA-Net achieves better performance than state-of-the-art methods.The SD-CSCD dataset and the code of SSDA-Net are available at https://github.com/XxxJ0909/SSDA-Net.

title: Performance Analysis of Quantum GKP Error Correction Code Under Noisy Environment
authors: M. Samin Salek, M.A.Mosabbir Antor, Md. Saifur Rahman
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence &amp; Networking (QPAIN)
abstract: Gottesman-Kitaev-Preskill (GKP) Codes tend to address one of the most challenging tasks for Quantum Communication of correcting the erroneous quantum bits. This paper focuses on analyzing the performance of this code under thermal displacement noise. GKP-encoded quantum memory under repeated error-correction cycles was simulated using the Strawberry Fields Bosonic simulator via finite energy approximation of the GKP state. We evaluate the logical-state fidelity as a function of correction cycles and noise strength, and analyze the effectiveness of error correction. Our results illustrate the robustness limits of GKP-based memory under realistic noise and highlight the dependence of logical performance on displacement noise variance, providing insight into practical continuous-variable quantum error correction.

title: MEV Detection for Ethereum Security: An Empirical Analysis on Agreement and Scalability
authors: Shraddha M. Naik, Huned Materwala, Davor Svetinovic
publish: IEEE Transactions on Emerging Topics in Computing
abstract: Maximal Extractable Value (MEV) poses significant threats to the security and fairness of Ethereum's decentralized finance ecosystem by enabling participants to exploit transaction ordering to extract profits at the expense of others. Heuristic-based detection methods have been widely adopted to identify MEV transactions such as sandwich, arbitrage, and liquidation. However, a lack of standardized evaluation across heuristics limits the ability to compare their detection behavior and computational characteristics. This paper presents a unified experimental framework to systematically evaluate the detection capabilities, agreement rates, and resource efficiency of existing heuristic approaches. An agreement metric is introduced to quantify consistency across detection methods. Additionally, we assessed resource utilization and execution time to evaluate computational scalability. Our empirical findings reveal that while these approaches exhibit scalability, their reliability, measured in terms of detection consistency, varies significantly across different MEV types. Agreement rates averaged 0.53 for sandwich detection, 0.40 for arbitrage, and 0.81 for liquidation, highlighting substantial differences in detection capabilities and heuristic formulations. These findings offer valuable insights into the practical challenges of achieving consistent MEV detection and highlight the need for developing more robust security countermeasures.

title: An Information Security Risk Assessment Method for Intelligent Space-Air Networks
authors: Zhaoguo Zhu, Zhicheng Xu, Xiaodong Ling, Zhidong Cao
abstract: Intelligent space-air networks are characterized by cross-domain heterogeneous interconnection, rapidly changing topology, link fluctuations, and constrained onboard resources, which make their information security risks highly coupled and dynamic. Existing studies have established security indicator systems for satellite Internet from the perspectives of functionality, performance, availability, reliability, and supportability, and have analyzed major threats at the physical, operational, and data layers. However, these studies mainly focus on security state description and lack a unified framework for quantitative and dynamic risk assessment. To address this issue, this paper proposes an information security risk assessment method for intelligent space-air networks. The proposed method first quantifies and normalizes multidimensional security indicators and combines the analytic hierarchy process with the entropy weight method to determine indicator weights. It then computes system security capability, maps vulnerability, and constructs a comprehensive risk model by integrating threat intensity and impact severity. On this basis, a dynamic correction mechanism is introduced by incorporating topology variation, link fluctuation, and real-time attack alerts. Four representative scenarios, namely normal operation, physical-layer disturbance, operational-layer attack, and data-layer compound attack, are designed for case-based validation. The results show that the proposed method can effectively distinguish risk differences across scenarios and capture the amplification effect of dynamic factors on risk values. The method can support risk warning and security protection for intelligent space-air networks.

title: Power System Control in DC Shipboard Power Systems: A Review of Methods and Architectures
authors: Timon Kopka, Andrea Coraddu, Henk Polinder
abstract: The electrification of shipboard power systems (SPSs), combined with the introduction of heterogeneous power sources and energy storage technologies, is driving a need for more advanced and structured control strategies. This review examines control methods and architectures for DC ships, with a specific interest in power systems integrating energy storage systems and zero-emission power generation. Control methods are categorized based on both their functionality and architecture, evaluating their resilience, adaptability, and scalability. Different hierarchical layers are reviewed, distinguishing local control, coordinated control, and energy management methods. Key challenge in the coordinated control arise due to large load fluctuations, constant-power loads, low inertia, and diverse dynamic capabilities of power sources and storage systems. These characteristics complicate voltage stability, dynamic power sharing, and state-of-charge management. Decentralized, centralized, and distributed control architectures are reviewed with respect to scalability, communication requirements, and fault tolerance. At the high-level layer, energy management strategies are discussed in terms of operational efficiency and resiliency, with predictive and distributed methods forming key trends in shipboard power system control. The review highlights the need for resilient, adaptive, and scalable control solutions tailored to future DC SPSs, particularly those integrating fuel cells and energy storage technologies.

title: Two-Stage Noise-Reduced Pneumonia Detection in Chest X-Ray Images using Denoising Autoencoder–CNN Integration
authors: Md Abdul Mutalib, Kopil Das, Mohammad Hossain, Shahriar Hussain, Avijit Deb Nath, Md Mahmud Zaman, Md. Alamgir Hossain
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence &amp; Networking (QPAIN)
abstract: Pneumonia remains a major cause of morbidity and mortality worldwide, with chest X-ray imaging serving as a primary tool for early diagnosis. However, the presence of noise, artifacts, and low-quality acquisitions significantly degrades image interpretability and limits the effectiveness of automated deep learning–based detection systems. Conventional convolutional neural networks (CNNs) are particularly sensitive to such degradations, leading to reduced classification reliability. To address this limitation, we propose a hybrid deep learning framework that integrates a denoising autoencoder (DAE) with a CNN for robust pneumonia detection from chest X-ray images. The DAE is trained to reconstruct clean images from noisy inputs, enabling the suppression of noise while preserving diagnostically relevant features, and is subsequently coupled with a CNN classifier in a unified pipeline. The proposed model is evaluated on the publicly available Kaggle chest X-ray dataset using data augmentation, class-weighted loss, and early stopping to mitigate class imbalance and overfitting. Experimental results demonstrate that the DAE-CNN framework consistently outperforms a CNN-only baseline, achieving an accuracy of 92.9%, an F1-score of 94.6%, and a balanced accuracy of 90.9%, compared to 86.1% accuracy and an F1-score of 89.8% obtained without denoising. These results highlight the effectiveness of incorporating learnable denoising into deep learning pipelines and underscore its potential for improving the robustness and reliability of automated pneumonia detection in clinical imaging scenarios.

title: Performance Analysis of 6T and 8T Sram Cells at Low Supply Voltage in 65-nm CMOS
authors: Abu Talha Haque Miah, A. S. M. Kibrya, Miss Al Ive, Ratin Islam
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence &amp; Networking (QPAIN)
abstract: This study presents a comparative analysis of six transistor and eight transistor SRAM cells implemented in 65 nm CMOS technology and simulated in LTspice across thirty supply voltage points ranging from 0.85 V to 1.5 V. Performance characteristics evaluated were access times during read/write operations, static noise margin (SNM), dynamic power and leakage power, and area efficiency under identical operating conditions. SNM was extracted using the conventional butterfly curve method; access delay measurements were taken from the 50% VDD point at the time of transition between active and inactive states. Based on simulation data, it was found that the 8T cell had 25% higher read SNM (125 mV vs 100 mV) and 17% shorter read access delay than the six-transistor cell (approximately 240 ps vs 290 ps), primarily due to the decoupled read path architecture of the eight-transistor cell to its sixtransistor equivalent. Although the six-transistor cell consumed lower dynamic power than the eight-transistor cell design (14%) and was more area efficient, these findings demonstrate the natural trade-offs between stability, speed, and area. Thus, this work is very important for low-voltage and energy-restricted applications.

title: MD-DGNN: A Metadata-Driven Dual Graph Neural Network based on Brain Functional Connectivity for ASD Classification
authors: Jing Li, Jinbei Zhang, Tao Leng, Bin Zhang
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Autism spectrum disorder (ASD) is a complex neurodevelopmental disorder that requires early and efficient diagnosis. Functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI), with high temporal and spatial resolution, is a powerful tool for investigating brain functional connectivity. In fMRI data-based ASD classification, the dual graph method integrates individual brain functional connectivity with metadata-based inter-subject relationships, achieving high diagnostic accuracy. However, relying on the predefined brain graphs with fixed structures in the brain graph learning stage fails to adaptively optimize graph topology for downstream tasks. Furthermore, existing methods perform early fusion of metadata to construct a single population graph, discarding critical metadata specific information and causing unclear inter-subject relationships. Accordingly, we propose a metadata-driven dual graph neural network (MD-DGNN) to alleviate these problems, which consists of an edge-adaptive GNN and a metadata-aware GNN to process the brain graphs and population graphs, respectively. In edge-adaptive GNN, we design a dynamic edge sampling module to adaptively learn the task-optimal graph topologies. In metadata-aware GNN, we construct heterogeneous population graphs based on different types of metadata and implement a hierarchical cross-fusion strategy for integrating metadata-specific features between branches. Experiments on publicly available ABIDE I and our self-collected dataset TJAD-ASD MRI reveal that the proposed MD-DGNN achieves the state of-the-art performance, demonstrating the superiority of our method.

title: Artificial Neural Network Model for Predicting OAM Mode Transmission Characteristics in Multicore Fiber
authors: Rosni Sayed, Abdul Khaleque, Sumaya Akter, Kumary Sumi Rani Shaha, Sadman Sakib, Shaila Alam Urmi
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence &amp; Networking (QPAIN)
abstract: This work presents a deep learning-assisted framework for the optical performance prediction of a six-core orbital angular momentum (OAM) mode supporting multicore fiber (MCF). An artificial neural network (ANN) model is developed and configured to map structural inputs to optical output characteristics, including supported mode count, effective refractive index, refractive index difference, chromatic dispersion, and crosstalk. The model achieves a low prediction error (mean square error = <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$7.11 \times 10^{-4}$</tex>), demonstrating strong agreement with finite element method (FEM) simulation results while requiring only a fraction of the computational cost. Compared to conventional COMSOL Multiphysics simulations, the proposed model significantly accelerates evaluation, making it suitable for rapid parameter exploration, optimization, and inverse fiber design. The demonstrated approach highlights the potential of deep learning as a fast, accurate, and resource-efficient tool for next-generation OAM mode supporting fiber development.

title: A Word-Level Aware Semantic Cross-Modal Comparison Model for 3D Textual Understanding
authors: Jinmeng Wu, Jingwei Ma, Yiming Tu, Hanyu Hong, Xia Hua, Ying Zhu, Jeyan Thiyagalingam
abstract: 3D semantic learning based on large models is a meaningful technology for analyzing and understanding the meaning of scenes. In 3D classification tasks, existing models have achieved impressive results using effective multimodal learning. However, these methods still rely on the effects of pre-trained models between external images and texts without considering 3D semantic characteristics. In this work, we design a word-level aware semantic cross-modal comparison turning model, abbreviated as WA3Dpoint, as the backbone method for 3D semantic learning. This paper first introduces the 3D semi-trained encoder for extracting point cloud features. Subsequently, the model constructs the cross-modal semantic comparison module that adapts to the various integration methods for interactive learning between text and point cloud. Finally, we adopt a step-wise training strategy to train 3D encoder separately and optimize the semantic features. The experimental results show that our method obtains an accuracy of 92.77% on the OBJ-ONLY of the ScanObjectNN dataset and 94.20% on the ModelNet40 dataset for the classification task, an average instance IoU of 86.10% on the ShapeNetPart dataset for part segmentation task. In particular, our method has performed competitive results in the task of few-shot point cloud classification compared to other state-of-the-art works.

title: Curriculum Domain Adaptation via Wavelet Mixup for Cyber–Physical–Social Intelligence
authors: Xuejun Cheng, Haiteng Wang, Zidi Jia, Lei Ren
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Social Systems
abstract: Cyber–physical–social intelligence (CPSI) requires robust perception models that generalize across heterogeneous human-centered sensing environments. However, sensor data in cyber–physical–social systems (CPSSs) often exhibit strong nonstationarity, low signal-to-noise ratios, and substantial cross-subject distribution shifts, which can severely degrade model performance. Existing adversarial unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA) methods mainly focus on feature-level alignment, but they often underuse target-domain structural information when training the task classifier, especially under large source–target discrepancies. To address this issue, we propose wavelet-enhanced curriculum domain adaptation (Wave-CDA), a curriculum-based UDA framework with wavelet-domain mixup for CPSS-relevant sensor time series. Wave-CDA consists of three stages: 1) adversarial UDA pretraining for learning transferable representations; 2) multilevel wavelet mixup (WMix) for generating intermediate-domain samples that preserve important time–frequency structures while incorporating unlabeled target information; and 3) transferability-guided curriculum training that progressively introduces augmented samples from easier-to-transfer samples to harder samples. We further design three transferability measures, namely label confidence, label agreement, and domain confusion, to assess the reliability and usefulness of augmented samples. Experiments on cross-subject emotion recognition, human activity recognition, and human gesture recognition show that Wave-CDA consistently outperforms competitive baselines. Additional analyses of ablation, transferability weighting, and training behavior further support the effectiveness of wavelet-domain augmentation and curriculum scheduling.

title: Human-Guided Autonomous Learning for UAV Air Combat Decision Optimization
authors: Shuai Hao, Haibin Duan, Chen Wei
publish: IEEE Systems, Man, and Cybernetics Magazine
abstract: Autonomous air-combat decision-making for unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) faces a critical challenge: pure reinforcement learning (RL) suffers from severe cold-start problems in high-dimensional adversarial environments, while imitation learning (IL) alone cannot exceed human performance. This paper proposes a two-stage human-guided learning framework that integrates generative adversarial imitation learning (GAIL) for policy initialization and proximal policy optimization (PPO) for subsequent refinement. In Stage 1, GAIL bootstraps a competent policy from only 2.7 hours of non-expert demonstrations, after which the UAV masters the complete tactical sequence of pursuit, lock-on, and engagement with substantially fewer samples than pure RL baselines. In Stage 2, PPO elevates performance beyond human-demonstrator levels. Validation in the Harfang 3D Dogfight high-fidelity simulator shows a 2.3- fold improvement in kill success rate and a 30 percent reduction in time-to-kill compared to state-of-the-art RL methods. These results indicate that modest human guidance can effectively overcome the cold-start barrier in adversarial continuous control, offering a practical pathway toward robust autonomous combat systems. The central finding is that GAIL enables the neural network to acquire the correct engagement sequence— establishing radar lock prior to weapon release—a fundamental combat discipline that pure RL did not discover within the tested training budgets.

title: A Programming Model for Efficient Inter-Kernel Control-Flow on Memory-Mapped Near-Data Processing Architecture (WIP)
authors: Seungheon Lee, Wonhyuk Yang, Seonyeong Heo, Gwangsun Kim
publish: Proceedings of the 27th ACM SIGPLAN/SIGBED International Conference on Languages, Compilers, and Tools for Embedded Systems

title: Identifying, Evaluating, and Mitigating Risks of AI Thought Partnerships

authors: Mukulita Som, Keerthi Vallikadevi Kalepu, Rajat Subhra Chakraborty

title: Using the Internal Operational Logs of the hinotori&#x2122; Surgical Robot System to Evaluate Latent Structural Metrics of Robotic Surgical Skill_supp1-3699228.docx
authors: Munenori Uemura
abstract: Surgical robot systems generate detailed kinematic telemetry, yet these internal logs remain underexplored as a resource for quantitative analysis of operative behavior. This study investigated whether motion features derived from maintenance-grade telemetry of the hinotori™ Surgical Robot System capture the characteristics of robotic surgical expertise. Thirty-nine participants—robotic surgeons, laparoscopic surgeons without robotic experience (Lap), and novices—performed a standardized suturing task while internal motion logs were recorded. Motion features, including conventional kinematic metrics and geometric coordination descriptors, were computed from instrument and endoscope trajectories. Feature importance was analyzed using multiple tree-based machine-learning models with repeated resampling. Across all models, spatial coordination features consistently ranked highest. The most robust feature was the Triangular Stability Index (TSI), which represented the geometric stability of the endoscope–instrument configuration. Robotic surgeons exhibited significantly lower and more stable TSI values than Lap and novice participants, indicating coordinated scope–arm control. These findings suggest that robotic expertise may be characterized by robot-specific visuomotor coordination rather than general surgical experience. Internal telemetry provides an objective and scalable foundation for behavioral analysis, surgical training, and adaptive robotic assistance.

title: AEVisionLab: Manipulating In-vehicle Ethernet Networks with All-round Vision
authors: Anthony Yeo, Matheus Garbelini, Sai Sathiesh Rajan, Jianying Zhou, Sudipta Chattopadhyay
