title: Efficient Split-Posit DSP Slice Architecture for Embedded FPGA Fabrics
authors: Arun M, Madhav Rao
publish: 2026 IEEE 34th Annual International Symposium on Field-Programmable Custom Computing Machines (FCCM)
abstract: This work introduces the Split-Posit format, designed to mimic the benefits of high-precision Posit values at significantly lower bit widths, achieving a favourable trade-off between accuracy and hardware efficiency. Furthermore, we propose a specialized digital signal processing (DSP) architecture for embedded FPGAs tailored for Split-Posit arithmetic, efficient and well-suited for its variable-length field structure.

title:
A Dual-Parameter Short-Circuit Protection Scheme for SiC MOSFETs Based on V
                    <sub>DS</sub>
                    and i
                    <sub>G</sub>
                    Detection
authors: Chenyan Li, Ziyue Zhu, Su Meng, Guolin Deng, Haihong Qin
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Compared with Si IGBTs, SiC MOSFETs offer faster switching speed, lower conduction losses, and better thermal management, enabling SiC-based solid-state circuit breakers to reduce device size and steady-state losses. However, their limited short-circuit withstand capability restricts the application of solid-state circuit breakers, necessitating reliable short-circuit protection. To address this, a dual-parameter short-circuit protection scheme for SiC MOSFETs based on drain-source voltage and gate current monitoring is proposed. Short-circuit fault mechanisms and two fault modes are analyzed, followed by simulation investigation of factors affecting gate current and drain-source voltage during short-circuit condition; operational principles of the short-circuit protection scheme and parameter optimization are then detailed. An experimental platform evaluates its hard switching fault and fault under load protection performance under different voltages, with protection speed compared to a desaturation detection circuit. Results show the proposed protection scheme achieves short-circuit detection within 550ns and protection within 900ns.

title: Evaluating Supply Chain Operational Efficiency in Game-Based Learning: A Data Envelopment Analysis of Student Performance
authors: Martin Flegl, Jaime A. Palma-Mendoza, Ivan A. Arana-Solares, Ernesto A. Pacheco-Velazquez
abstract: Empirical investigations into the evolution of student decision-making efficiency within supply chain education remain limited, despite the growing adoption of gamified simulations. This study applies Data Envelopment Analysis (DEA) with a Window Analysis approach to evaluate the supply chain operational efficiency achieved by postgraduate students using Logistics Simulator (LOST) a game-based supply chain simulation during the initial period of an elective course (September cohort). The simulation demonstrated an initial increase in efficiency due to optimized procurement and transportation, followed by significant fluctuations and sharp declines linked to inadequate production planning, ultimately resulting in an average demand compliance rate of only 66.37%. These findings underscore the importance of cultivating anticipatory skills and systems thinking in educational curricula to overcome persistent inefficiencies in cross-functional coordination and production alignment. The study affirms the instrumental role of dynamic simulation environments in fostering critical competencies in resource allocation and risk awareness, essential to modern supply chain management.

title: Electrocardiogram Acquisition System and Cardiac Frequency Estimation Implemented in FPGA and Tested using FPGA in-the-Loop
authors: Vitória Dos Santos Fahed, Raymundo Cordero, João Carlos Machado, Matheus Silva Santos
publish: 2026 Global Medical Engineering Physics Exchanges/ Pan American Health Care Exchanges (GMEPE/PAHCE)
abstract: Electrocardiogram (ECG) signals are produced by the electrical activity of the heart. These signals can be acquired through electrodes and analyzed for disease diagnosis, heart rate determination, and other medical information. Interference from electrical grid, muscles and noise can affect the ECG signal. This paper presents an ECG signal acquisition system and an algorithm for cardiac frequency estimation, both implemented in FPGA (Field Programmable Gate Array). FPGA has high potential to develop ECG analysis systems by its flexible architecture and parallel processing. This paper also makes use of FPGA in-the-Loop (FIL) method to test the results of the cardiac frequency estimation. FIL allows testing an FPGA algorithm using testing signals produced by a numerical software program executed in a computer. Thus, FIL allows proving the accuracy of the estimation algorithm under controlled and known circumstances, while the hardware used to test the FPGA is simplified. Experimental tests show that the proposed approach is suitable for ECG analysis, and FIL testing method allows a useful visualization of the signals used in the proposed estimation method. It is proposed that FIL can be used to debug and verify other biomedical algorithms implemented in FPGA.

title: Comparison of Characteristics between Variable Frequency Transformer and Rotary Power Flow Controller
authors: Baiyan Sun, Zehao Xu, Hang Zhang
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: A promising solution to improve the reliability of the distribution network is employing interconnection devices to realize power flow reallocation in distribution systems with large-scale integration of the renewable distributed sources into distribution networks. Compared with flexible interconnection devices based on power electronics, variable frequency transformer (VFT) and rotary power flow controller (RPFC) offer lower cost, higher reliability, and superior overload capability while achieving power flow regulation. The operating principles of RPFC and VFT are introduced in the paper. A comparative analysis is conducted in terms of power coupling characteristics, operating characteristics under distribution network faults, capacity characteristics, loss characteristics and techno-economic performance, thereby clarifying the applicable scenarios of each. VFT features large capacity and high cost while RPFC features low capacity and low cost, which is caused by the different operating principles of the two devices. The VFT is applicable to a broader range of scenarios compared with the RPFC.

title: A Proposal for 3D Electro-Anatomical Mapping Software Derived from the Comparison of Systematic Benchmarking of Clinical Systems
authors: Shiva Eghdamian, Jose Carlos Pachon, João Salinet
publish: 2026 Global Medical Engineering Physics Exchanges/ Pan American Health Care Exchanges (GMEPE/PAHCE)
abstract: Three-dimensional Electro-anatomical Mapping systems (3D EAMs) are the primary platforms worldwide for guiding catheter ablation in the treatment of cardiac arrhythmias. Despite their clinical effectiveness, current 3D EAMs are proprietary, costly, and limit accessibility. In this study, for the first time in Latin America, it is proposed to outline how the core software components of a low-cost 3D EAM could be designed for the Brazilian Unified Health System (SUS). The proposed software is based on a structured benchmarking analysis of four commercial 3D EAMs: Carto (Johnson & Johnson, Biosense Webster), EnSite (Abbott), Rhythmia (Boston Scientific), and AcQMap (Acutus Medical). For each system, the following items were studied: user manual and technical tutorials, peer-reviewed literature, and assistance in 60 catheter ablation procedures. The results highlight variability across commercial platforms in catheter localization accuracy, signal acquisition, processing, and annotation strategies, supporting the need for greater standardization. The proposed system will support hybrid catheter localization, including unipolar and bipolar intracardiac electrograms (EGMs) and 12-lead ECG signals with sampling frequency of 2 kHz. Signal pre-processing includes band-pass filtering (HPF 0.05–40 Hz, LPF 10–500 Hz) and 60 Hz notch filtering, with voltage and local activation time mapping modalities.

title: Min-Tx-Power Joint Optimization for RSMA-UAV Multicast
authors: Dongzhi Liu, Dongyu Wang
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Information and Network Technologies (ICINT)
abstract: Rate-Splitting Multiple Access (RSMA) splits each user’s message into a common stream and a private stream, employing successive interference cancellation (SIC) for efficient signal decoding. Unlike conventional non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA), RSMA eliminates idle slots and relaxes strict power allocation constraints. In post-disaster scenarios, battery-powered unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) must serve numerous users with limited energy. This scheme jointly optimizes 3D UAV deployment and RSMA precoding to minimize transmit power while guaranteeing each user’s target data rate. An alternating optimization framework is proposed: UAV positioning is solved via an enhanced particle swarm optimization (PSO) algorithm, while precoder design is obtained through successive convex approximation (SCA) with CVX. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed RSMA scheme achieves a reduction in UAV transmit power compared to baseline RSMA, NOMA, and orthogonal multiple access (OMA) schemes.

title: Optimization Method for PV-ESS-H2 System Configuration Integrating Physical Constraints and Neural Networks
authors: Yuhui Wu, Yanlun Zhang, Shouyuan Tao, Xi Huang, Zeyu Shi, Zhifeng He
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Aiming at the problems of complex calculation and strong data dependence in the configuration optimization of photovoltaic-energy storage-hydrogen production (PV-ESS-H2) systems, a fast optimization method integrating deterministic physical rules and a two-stage neural network is proposed. This method generates training samples by constructing a deterministic calculation engine, designs a two-stage neural network surrogate model with embedded physical constraints, and achieves efficient optimization through a collaborative mechanism of "rapid screening - accurate verification". Case studies show that the proposed method greatly improves optimization efficiency while ensuring accuracy, and is suitable for the engineering planning stage of PV-ESS-H2 system design.

title: A Hybrid Cluster-Based Classification Model for Anomaly Detection in Unbalanced IoT Networks
authors: Hossein Shaemi Barzoki, Amir Hossein Fathi Hafshejani, Ahmadreza Montazerolghaem
publish: 2025 11th International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Systems (ICSPIS)
abstract: Detecting anomalies in Internet of Things (IoT) networks is a critical security challenge, often hampered by highly imbalanced and diverse network traffic datasets. Standard classifiers struggle to perform well across all traffic types. This paper proposes a hybrid detection model to address this challenge using the Bot-IoT dataset. Instead of a single complex classifier, we first employ K-Means clustering to segment the training data into three distinct traffic profile clusters. We then train and evaluate multiple baseline machine learning models, including Decision Tree, KNN, and XGBoost, on each cluster independently to identify the optimal classifier for that specific data profile. Our results show that this cluster-specific, hybrid approach, which assigns different simple models to different clusters, improves detection accuracy and provides a more robust and efficient framework for handling diverse IoT attack traffic.

title: Dynamic Incentive Strategy for DERs in Distribution Network Congestion Management
authors: Ruixuan Zhuang, Yulong Jin, Bokang Zou
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: With the large-scale integration of distributed energy resources (DER), balancing supply and demand in power systems has become increasingly complex. Without effective coordination, the concentrated and uncoordinated charging, discharging, and load regulation of massive flexibility resources can significantly impact the stability of distribution networks(DN). This may cause transmission congestion, threatening the safe operation of the power grid. To address this, this work proposes a dynamic incentive strategy for regulating flexibility resources to manage DN congestion. First, a collaborative simulation model is constructed to comprehensively account for the regulation characteristics of diverse DERs, the topology of the DN, and its mapping relationship with the transportation network. Second, an optimization model for the incentive pricing strategy is developed based on a sensitivity analysis of the DN parameters. Finally, case simulations are conducted to verify the effectiveness of the proposed strategy.

title: Integration of Artificial Intelligence in Engineering Education: Student Perceptions of NotebookLM
authors: Jorge Alvarez, José M. Nieto-Jalil, Adrian Isrrael Tec Chim, David I. Ibarra-Zarate, Andrei Solórzano Pérez, Juan Manuel Martinez Huerta
abstract: This study explores the impact of Google NotebookLM, an artificial intelligence-based tool, on the learning experience of first-year engineering students. In a controlled academic setting, 91 students used NotebookLM to carry out tasks involving the synthesis of technical information and subsequently evaluated their experience using the UEQ-S (User Experience Questionnaire – Short Version), supplemented with open-ended questions. Quantitative results reveal a positive perception across pragmatic dimensions (clarity, efficiency, usefulness) and hedonic dimensions (interest, novelty, motivation). Qualitative comments highlight the tool's ability to summarize and organize complex content, as well as its potential to foster autonomous learning. Although limitations were identified, such as slow processing and interface design issues, most participants expressed willingness to reuse NotebookLM in future academic projects. This work provides preliminary evidence of the value of integrating AI tools into engineering education, particularly in early stages of training, and suggests future research directions regarding its longitudinal impact and comparison with other educational technologies.

title: DDPG-Based Duty-Cycle Control for Buck DC–DC Converters With Fast Voltage Regulation Under Parameter Variations
authors: Weide Guan, Yingxi Zhu, Yang Zhou, Su Liu, Peiling Jiang, Zhenbang Wang, Zihao Liu, Yang Chen
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Fast and robust voltage regulation of Buck DC – DC converters remains challenging under operating-point changes and parameter variations, where conventional PI-based closed-loop control may exhibit sluggish transient response and degraded robustness. This paper proposes a DDPG-based duty-cycle controller that directly outputs the continuous-duty action through an Actor – Critic architecture, avoiding model linearization and enabling nonlinear control-law learning. The controller is trained offline and deployed via lightweight online inference to meet high-frequency switching real-time requirements. In the proposed formulation, the observation vector includes the voltage error, integral of error, and output voltage, while reward function penalizes voltage-tracking error and inductor-current stress to encourage fast regulation with constrained current dynamics. MATLAB/Simulink studies verify that the trained agent achieves fast, well-damped voltage regulation under reference steps, input-voltage sags, abrupt load changes, Inductor changes and capacitor changes. The performance remains robust under inductance/capacitance variations, indicating strong tolerance to plant uncertainties.

title: Etching Depth Measurement for 3D NAND Process Control Through CD-SAXS Metrology
authors: Ming-Tsung Wu, Min-Hsuan Huang, Yi-Che Chen, Yi-Han Chao, Cheng-En Tsai, Hsiao-Fei Su, Tien-Jung Lee, Hyowon Park, Ramkumar Subramanian
publish: 2026 37th Annual SEMI Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing Conference (ASMC)
abstract: In advanced 3D NAND flash memory fabrication, precise control of channel hole depth is critical for ensuring uniform electrical characteristics and reliable device performance. Recent studies have shown that increasing stack height amplifies etch non-uniformity and pattern-edge effects in channel holes. Variations in etch depth across the wafer can lead to "under-etch" or "over-etch," risking incomplete channel formation or punch-through failures, respectively. These challenges are exacerbated by the limited accessibility of deeply buried features, which restricts the capability of traditional cross-section SEM (X-SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) methods for high-volume metrology. Conventional techniques like X-SEM/TEM are destructive, time-consuming, and limited to localized sampling, making them unsuitable for large-scale process monitoring. To address these limitations, we present a non-destructive approach based on Critical Dimension Small-Angle X-ray Scattering (CD-SAXS) from KLA’s Axion® system. CD-SAXS has recently been identified as a promising next-generation inline metrology solution capable of characterizing 3D-NAND channel holes. This method leverages the sensitivity of scattering profiles to vertical structural dimensions, extracting depth information through an advanced modeling approach from KLA. This approach enables rapid process feedback, reduces metrology costs, and supports robust inline depth control in 3D NAND manufacturing.

title: LSTM-Based Emotional EEG Framework for Alzheimer’s Diagnosis Using Connectivity and CNN Features
authors: Arshia Rezaei, Sargol Moslemi
publish: 2025 11th International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Systems (ICSPIS)
abstract: Electroencephalography (EEG) signals enable reliable, non-invasive, real-time monitoring of brain activity and hold significant potential for diagnosing neurological disorders. Recent studies focused on Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) models in classifying cognitive and emotional states from EEG data. However, many existing approaches directly input high-dimensional raw EEG signals into CNN architectures, often overlooking complex spatiotemporal dependencies and affective dynamics. To overcome this issue, a deep framework is proposed that integrates brain connectivity with emotion-aware representations by combining CNN-based spatial feature extraction with Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)-based temporal modeling. By leveraging connectivity patterns and the sequential learning capability of LSTM networks, the framework captures discriminative neurophysiological markers critical for early Alzheimer’s detection. Experimental results demonstrate superior accuracy, interpretability, and robustness compared with conventional baselines. These findings highlight the importance of jointly incorporating emotional context and connectivity for accurate, interpretable, and reliable EEG-based Alzheimer’s diagnosis, achieving an accuracy of 93.475%. Furthermore, the model is deployed within a user-friendly Graphical User Interface (GUI), enhancing usability and facilitating seamless clinical integration.

title: Improving BGP-iSec against attacks and accidents
authors: Keishi Hashiba, Shingo Okamura
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Information and Network Technologies (ICINT)
abstract: In recent years, BGP-iSec, a security mechanism as an extension of BGP security utilizing RPKI, has been proposed. This protocol is designed to prevent route hijacks and route leaks, which are difficult to prevent only with ROA/ROV. However, even with BGP-iSec, there are still attack and accidents that are difficult to prevent. In this study, we identify these issues such that BGP-iSec cannot prevent. We also present an improvement proposal that take into account existing issues, and discuss its effectiveness.

title: Federated Learning for Semiconductor Digital Twins: A Privacy-Preserving Framework for Collaborative DUV Nanolithography
authors: Haiyang Yun, Hao Chun Liao, Benyamin Davaji, Peter C. Doerschuk, Amit Lal
publish: 2026 37th Annual SEMI Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing Conference (ASMC)
abstract: We present a federated learning (FL) framework for privacy-preserving collaborative training of a conditional generative adversarial network (cGAN) applied to Deep UV nanolithography digital twins. Using the Flower platform with FedAvg aggregation, multiple clients collaboratively train a deep learning model to translate mask layouts into synthetic SEM images without sharing manufacturing and metrology data. We evaluate two federated split configurations: balanced (50/50) and imbalanced (30/70), against centralized and local-only baselines across a dataset of 7,844 CD-SEM image pairs spanning six nanostructure types representing building blocks for in-plane NEMS switch. The FL 50/50 global model achieves a PSNR of 19.87 dB (vs. 19.25 dB centralized) and a width MAE of 4.54 nm (vs. 5.01 nm centralized), consistently matching or outperforming both centralized and local-only models, with notable gains in image quality (PSNR) and width accuracy. These results demonstrate that federated learning preserves and even enhances predictive quality while ensuring data confidentiality, providing a viable pathway for collaborative, cross-foundry digital twin development in advanced semiconductor manufacturing.

title: Estimation of Ultrasonic Transmission Coefficients at Plastisol Interfaces for Ultrasonic Phamtom
authors: J. Oliveira, W. C. A. Pereira
publish: 2026 Global Medical Engineering Physics Exchanges/ Pan American Health Care Exchanges (GMEPE/PAHCE)
abstract: This study estimates ultrasonic intensity transmission coefficients (T) at interfaces between PVC-based plastisol formulations (Ultra, Soft, and Standard) to support the development of multilayer ultrasonic phantoms using a domestically produced material that replaces a previously imported formulation. Beyond improving material availability, the national formulation may reduce procurement costs and facilitate wider access to phantom fabrication. Samples were prepared under controlled thermal conditions to ensure homogeneity, complete gelation, and reduced bubble content, and were characterized in terms of sound speed, density, attenuation, and acoustic impedance. Transmission coefficients were determined using two approaches: (1) an indirect impedance-based method and (2) an experimental amplitude-based method using transmitted peak-to-peak signals in coupled sample pairs, with attenuation and thickness corrections. The impedance-based approach predicted T values close to 1.0 for all interfaces, indicating high acoustic compatibility and negligible reflection under normal-incidence assumptions. In contrast, the amplitude-based approach exhibited higher dispersion and T > 1, consistent with the influence of non-ideal experimental factors, including coupling variability, multiple reflections, alignment, and geometric nonuniformity. Overall, the impedance-based method provides a stable theoretical baseline, whereas the amplitude-based method better represents the effective transmission behavior of the experimental system.

title: Coordinated Optimization of Heterogeneous Regulation Resources for Frequency-Stabilized Unit Commitment
authors: Shixu Deng, Li Ma, Liye Xiao, Shuwei Li
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: The high penetration of renewable energy has introduced significant challenges to power system security due to inherent variability and reduced system inertia. This paper proposes a novel unit commitment model incorporating frequency stability constraints with multiple heterogeneous regulation resources, including synchronous generators (SGs), synchronous condensers (SCs), battery energy storage systems (BESS), and electric-to-thermal molten salt energy storage (E2T-MSES). Key stability metrics for inertial and primary frequency responses are extracted, and frequency stability constraints are derived accounting for dynamic inertia and frequency regulation coefficients of storage units. To address wind power uncertainty, a scenario-based stochastic approach is adopted, reformulating the model as a two-step stochastic optimization framework. The first step determines unit commitment and baseline dispatch decisions, while the second step handles wind power uncertainty via stochastic programming. The coupling mechanisms and complementary characteristics between BESS and E2T-MSES are analyzed at the dispatch level, demonstrating how heterogeneous storage integration affects conventional generator and condenser allocation. Case studies validate the effectiveness of the proposed approach in enhancing both economic performance and frequency security.

title: NetCilk: Extending Task-Level Parallelism Seamlessly across FPGAs
authors: Mohamed Shahawy, Canberk Sönmez, Paolo Ienne
publish: 2026 IEEE 34th Annual International Symposium on Field-Programmable Custom Computing Machines (FCCM)
abstract: Task-level parallelism (TLP) is a long-standing approach in parallelizing control-dominated algorithms in software and has recently drawn interest in hardware acceleration thanks to the simplicity of managing tasks in hardware. Several TLP frameworks for FPGAs, like ParallelXL and HardCilk, leverage high-level synthesis to facilitate the acceleration of efficient TLP algorithms on FPGAs. In particular, HardCilk managed to efficiently deploy up to 256 processing elements (PEs) on a single FPGA system for microbenchmarks. However, for practical applications, it is usually not possible to fit more than a dozen PEs per FPGA, a limitation that reduces the potential of TLP. In this work, we propose an architecture that exploits the latency insensitivity of TLP and the high-bandwidth networking available in modern FPGAs to increase the number of available PEs for a given workload. We show that it is possible to span multiple FPGAs without loss of efficiency and with only a small resource overhead. We conduct experiments that distribute 128 PEs among two, four, and eight FPGAs and compare the efficiency with that of a single FPGA HardCilk system for a representative set of benchmarks. Our results accentuate the importance of an effective task movement policy across FPGAs to achieve scalability, particularly under specific network latency and bandwidth configurations. We also develop three real-world applications and test with two FPGAs setup, achieving almost perfect scalability. Our architecture achieves multi-FPGA extension with less than 3% of additional resource utilization.

title: Research on Arc Suppression Method Based on HMMC-SSFCL to Mitigate DC Neutral Point Voltage Drift
authors: Yike Li, Kun Yu, Xiyang Zhang, Jin Lu
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: With the rapid development of distributed energy resources and the integration of new energy into the grid, traditional AC distribution networks are evolving into hybrid AC/DC distribution networks. These systems, which incorporate DC buses and bidirectional converters (such as MMC and DAB), enable flexible energy exchange and efficient power management between AC and DC sides. However, single-phase-to-ground faults remain a significant challenge in these networks. Such faults can cause increased zero-sequence voltage, overvoltage on non-faulted phases, and resonance risks, which could lead to insulation failure and equipment damage. In response, arc suppression technologies have become essential for improving system safety and reliability. This paper proposes an arc suppression method based on Hybrid Modulation Modular Converters (HMMC) and Solid-State Fault Current Limiters (SSFCL). The method addresses the issue of DC-side neutral voltage drift caused by zero-sequence voltage injection during arc suppression. The study demonstrates that HMMC-SSFCL can quickly suppress fault currents and voltages during single-phase ground faults while also mitigating DC-side neutral point voltage drift, ensuring the stability and safe operation of hybrid AC/DC distribution systems.

title: Sustainable Computing: State-of-the-Art and Future Outlook
authors: N/A
abstract: None

title: POLAR: Polarization-Aware Object Localization and Recognition Framework for Concealed Threats in Passive Millimeter-Wave Images
authors: Li Zhang, Yayun Cheng, Pengcheng Wang, Nannan Wang, Jiaran Qi
abstract: As a subband of the terahertz spectrum, passive millimeter-wave (PMMW) imaging is ideal for human security screening due to nonionizing radiation and clothing-penetrating capabilities, but its poor image quality limits concealed threat recognition accuracy. To address this, we exploit polarization—a fundamental property of electromagnetic waves—as a rich source of complementary information to differentiate between the human body and concealed objects. This article proposes a polarization-aware object localization and recognition (POLAR) framework to construct the deep learning framework for PMMW imaging detection. The core innovation is a masked deformable convolution mechanism that integrates physically derived polarization masks to guide the spatial attention. These masks are computed from Stokes parameters extracted from four linear polarization (0<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^\circ$</tex-math></inline-formula>, 45<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^\circ$</tex-math></inline-formula>, 90<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^\circ$</tex-math></inline-formula>, 135<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^\circ$</tex-math></inline-formula>) brightness temperature images, effectively highlighting key regions with high contrast in each polarization modality. The POLAR adopts a multiscale processing pipeline, where multipolarization features at each scale are fused using dual-dimensional weights from both channel and spatial domains, and the recognition results are obtained based on the multiscale fused features. Compared with existing state-of-the-art methods, our method achieves the best overall performance while simultaneously maintaining high accuracy in comprehensive identification and high computational efficiency. The work demonstrates that embedding polarization physics into a deformable convolution framework significantly enhances the accuracy and interpretability of concealed threat detection in low-quality PMMW imagery.

title: Charging Station Pricing and Scheduling with Localized Personal Information via Offloading Computation to EVs
authors: Hongyi Wu, Xiaoying Tang, Ying-jun Angela Zhang
abstract: Rapid electric vehicle (EV) adoption has fueled the swift growth of charging station (CS) networks. Nevertheless, these stations encounter challenges in managing unpredictable user behavior while safeguarding EV owners’ privacy. This study introduces a Master-Follower Collaborative Reinforcement Learning (MFCRL) approach to facilitate the cooperative operation of multiple charging stations (CSs). Within the MFCRL framework, cooperative CSs assume the role of master, while EVs operate as followers. The master offloads training tasks involving private internal states to the followers and conducts training using a globally collected dataset, which remains resilient to unpredictable CS occupancy and aggregates local model updates from the followers. Consequently, RL models trained under this framework demonstrate robust performance by effectively adapting to dynamic electricity prices and variable station occupancy. Experimental results confirm the algorithm’s convergence and effectiveness in maximizing cumulative reward.

title: RGB-based Category-Level Object Pose Estimation with Multi Pose Maps for Robotic Grasp Detection
authors: Sheng Yu, Di-Hua Zhai, Yunqiao Zeng, Yuanqing Xia
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: RGB-D-based category-level object pose estimation has achieved very good pose estimation results in robotic grasp tasks. However, these methods rely on accurate depth information, and in industrial scenes where depth information is unknown or subject to significant interference, these methods are unable to apply. Therefore, this paper investigates the problem of category-level object pose estimation based solely on RGB images. First, to accurately predict the Normalized Object Coordinate Space (NOCS) map of objects in the scene, we build an encoding-decoding structure to achieve accurate NOCS map prediction. Then, to ensure an accurate transformation from NOCS maps to Intra-class Variation-Free Consensus (IVFC) maps, we propose a new deformable convolution that reduces the network’s computational load while improving the prediction accuracy of the IVFC maps. Finally, to fully utilize the information contained in multiple pose maps (NOCS maps and IVFC maps), we propose a Multi-Pose Map-based Pose (MPMPose) computation method to accurately predict the object pose. We test our method on the CAMERA25, REAL275, and Wild6D datasets, and the experimental results show that our proposed MPMPose can effectively complete the pose estimation task of unknown objects in scenes based solely on RGB images. Finally, we apply MPMPose to the robot grasping task in a real-world scenario. The experimental results show that MPMPose can effectively assist robots in completing the pose estimation task of objects in real scenes, enabling stable object grasping by robots.

title: Dual-Agent Self-Refinement for Scalable Transcript-Based Summarization
authors: Ajay Pendem, K. Silpaja Chandrasekar
abstract: An AI agent autonomously perceives, reasons, and executes tasks with minimal oversight. Existing summarization methods suffer from static pipelines and lack domain adaptation, while our multi-agent framework delivers dynamic, scalable performance. This study presents a tool-agnostic agentic framework for scalable transcript-based summarization through three novel contributions: classification-guided prompt adaptation, confidence-weighted chunking, and dual-agent self-refinement. The pipeline integrates language model capabilities for transcript extraction, classification, and summarization. It classifies domains using transformer-based zero-shot classification and dynamically adapts summarization prompts. A confidence-weighted chunking mechanism prioritizes salient segments, while a dual-agent critic-refinement loop evaluates and improves summaries until quality thresholds are met. We validate the framework on TVSum and SumMe benchmarks adapted for transcript-based evaluation, achieving competitive performance against state-of-the-art methods. The framework demonstrates robust agentic reasoning and processes transcripts in under 3 seconds on standard consumer hardware, providing a reproducible and scalable approach for automated content summarization.

title: Gas Generation Characteristics and Mechanisms of Dodecylbenzene Insulating Oil under Long-Term Thermal Aging
authors: Zhi Zhang, Jianyi Wang, Tengteng Niu, Xining Li, Xiankang Liao, Hao Tang, Ying Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: Certain ultra-high voltage AC oil-impregnated paper bushings using dodecylbenzene (DDB) as insulating oil exhibit a significantly higher proportion of ethane generation compared to other types of insulating oils, while the gas generation characteristics and mechanism of DDB under long-term thermal aging have not been fully elucidated. In this study, an integrated experimental and computational chemistry approach is employed to investigate the long-term thermal aging gas generation characteristics and mechanism properties of DDB oil. The results reveal that C<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>H<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">6</sub> is the main gas product, accompanied by minor quantities of CH<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">4</sub>, C<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>H<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">4</sub>, H<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>, and CO. The atomic-level mechanisms of ethane formation are clarified by reactive molecular dynamics (RMD) and density functional theory (DFT) simulations. It is verified that the C<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>H<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">5</sub>· produced by the decomposition of saturated alkane segments in DDB oil generates ethane by extracting the hydrogen atom at the benzylic position of the benzene ring, which is attributed to the low bond energy of the C-H bond at this location. The simulations further confirm that C<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>H<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">6</sub> formation is favored over C<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>H<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">4</sub> production under operating conditions due to thermodynamic spontaneity. By leveraging the high reactivity of C<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>H<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">5</sub>· intermediates, the proposed reaction pathway is validated through RMD simulations conducted at relatively lower temperatures. These findings provide theoretical support for the condition monitoring and maintenance strategies for ultra-high voltage oil-immersed paper bushings.

title: FedEPL+:Carbon-Aware Client Selection With Valid Differential Privacy in Federated Learning
authors: Hussain Mohammad Ricon, Tabassum Alam, Md. Tanzeem Rahat, Shakila Rahman, Kamruddin Nur
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence &amp; Networking (QPAIN)
abstract: The generation of large volumes of data due to smart city surveillance needs distributed learning solutions that are benign in data leaks and carbon cost. However, current privacypreserving FL frameworks often treat privacy and climate goals as opposing objectives. FedEPL+ (Federated Eco-Pprivate Learning +) is proposed with adaptive central differential privacy, emission-aware client scheduling and hierarchical aggregation. A Multi-Armed Bandit (MAB) scheduler matches computation with low-carbon energy windows. It also comes with a strict adaptive gradient clipping. FedEPL+ incorporates a robust Rényi Differential Privacy (RDP) accounting mechanism to address the analytic deficiencies of traditional privacy amplification outcomes in the context of adaptive, emission-sensitive participant selection. It is calibrated considering maximum inclusion probability (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$q_{\max }$</tex>), which provides a strong safeguard against underestimating privacy loss caused by data-driven scheduling. On CIFAR-10 simulation experiments, the accuracy of FedEPL+ is <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$86.0 \pm 2.0 \%$</tex> for strict privacy budgets (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\epsilon=3.0, \delta=10^{-5}$</tex>). The estimated carbon emission of FedEPL+ is <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{3 7. 5 \%}$</tex> smaller than private baselines. Moreover, the client starvation variance is only 0.08.

title: Mobility-Aware Sustainable Federated Learning via Auction Mechanisms in Vehicular Edge Computing
authors: Genqi Liu, Xiuhua Li, Jinlong Hao, Guozeng Xu, Xiaofei Wang, Victor C. M. Leung
abstract: Vehicular edge computing is rapidly amplifying the need to process computation-intensive tasks generated in vehicular environments. Conventional centralized processing frameworks struggle to meet these low-latency demands due to network latency and bandwidth constraints. Federated learning (FL) addresses these challenges by retaining raw data on vehicle nodes (VNs). However, sustainable FL training in vehicular edge computing faces persistent obstacles: the instability of VNs (high entry/exit rates), resource heterogeneity, and the lack of effective incentives. Notably, while existing incentive mechanisms are extensively studied, they inadequately address two critical sustainability barriers: extreme data heterogeneity and the volatile energy costs of mobile VNs. To overcome these challenges, we propose an auction-based sustainable federated learning (ASFL) framework. In this framework, the edge server acts as both the FL task publisher and the auctioneer, while VNs serve as bidders. Each bid encapsulates critical attributes including data quality, computing capacity, and projected energy cost. The core objective of ASFL is to maximize long-term social welfare. Formulating this objective reveals an inherently nonconvex optimization problem. Through rigorous analysis, we derive an equivalent convex formulation. The systematic bidder selection process inherent in ASFL simultaneously mitigates data heterogeneity and promotes rational energy utilization across FL. We theoretically prove that the framework achieves incentive compatibility and individual rationality. Experimental results on MNIST and CIFAR-10 datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the method in mitigating the impact of non-i.i.d. data and reducing energy consumption.

title: Analyzing and Predicting Voice Search Query Success by Integrating Sentiment Polarity, Text, and Behavioral Features
authors: Anika Rahman, Purbasha Halder, Madhure Rani Mondal
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence &amp; Networking (QPAIN)
abstract: Voice-based search is increasingly used for information retrieval, yet predicting the success of a voice query remains challenging due to the interplay of linguistic, behavioral, and system-level factors. This study presents a multimodal machine learning framework that integrates textual features, sentiment polarity, and behavioral metrics to classify query outcomes. Textual content is represented using TF-IDF vectors, sentiment polarity is derived from the query text, and behavioral features-including query duration, word count, device type, language, intent, and confidence-are encoded for prediction. Experiments were conducted on a dataset of 1,555 voice search queries. Six classifiers-Logistic Regression, Random Forest, XGBoost, LightGBM, Naive Bayes, and SVM-were evaluated using cross-validation, with Logistic Regression achieving the best performance, reaching an accuracy of 81%. Feature importance analysis revealed that query_duration_sec, confidence_score, intent, and query_text have the highest impact on predicting query success. These results demonstrate that combining behavioral and semantic signals significantly improves voice query performance prediction, enabling real-time optimization in conversational AI systems.

title: Data-Driven Machine Learning Approaches for Predicting the Compressive Strength of Ultra-High-Performance Concrete with the Influence of Fly Ash, and Blast Furnace Slag
authors: Samir Mazumder, Nahid Montasir Rifat, Nikhil Ronjon Roy, Arpon Roy, Rifat Mehjabin Aury, Komol Bhowmik, Md. Kawsar Akon, Hillol Sarkar, Proma Mondol
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence &amp; Networking (QPAIN)
abstract: Ultra-high-performance concrete (UHPC) is a sustainable, durable, and strong material. In this work, 359 experimental data are used for predicting its compressive strength (CS) using machine learning (ML). OPC, fly ash, blast furnace slag, coarse and fine aggregates, water, superplasticizers, and curing age were among the variables used in the mixed designs. Different model analysis parameters like mean absolute error (MAE), root mean square error (RMSE), coefficient of efficiency (CE), and coefficient of determination (R2) were used to assess four models such as Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost), Gradient Boosting (GB), Categorical Boosting (CatBoost), and Extra Trees. SHAP (Shapley Additive Explanations) and PDP (Partial Dependency Plot) analysis, the most important variables affecting strength were water, OPC, coarse aggregates, and curing age were measured. Reliability was attained and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0}$</tex>-fold cross-validation was used to avoid overfitting. Feature analysis and model comparisons provide useful information for improving UHPC performance and composition. The study provides insightful suggestions for improving UHPC mix design and enhancing sustainability through the use of industrial byproducts.

title: An Integrated Vision- and Environment-Aware Decision-Support System for Smart Harvesting
authors: Mridul Devnath, MD. Abdullah Al Shoab, Rifat Siddique, Symon Islam Parash, MD. Shahriar Jaman, Rumaiya Rashid Ema, MD. Sajid Hossain
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence &amp; Networking (QPAIN)
abstract: Decisions related to harvest timing and crop condition are still largely based on visual inspection and experience in many small- and medium-scale agricultural settings. Such practices make it difficult to respond consistently to changes in fruit maturity or environmental stress. Although digital tools have been proposed to support these tasks, most existing solutions focus on a single aspect, such as ripeness detection, environmental monitoring, or yield estimationwithout providing a unified basis for on-field decision making. This paper describes a locally deployable decision-support system that integrates visual assessment of fruit ripeness with environmental context and simple economic reasoning. Fruit images are analyzed to determine ripeness categories and associated confidence levels, which are then used to derive a relative yield potential indicator. In parallel, temperature and humidity measurements are interpreted through rule-based agronomic conditions to identify possible stress or diseasefavorable environments. The system favors transparent logic over black-box prediction, allowing users to understand how conclusions are formed. Operation is confined to a local area network using embedded sensing and vision at the edge, ensuring low latency and independence from continuous cloud access. By supporting more informed harvest decisions and reducing avoidable losses, the proposed system contributes toward Sustainable Development Goal 2 (Zero Hunger) and Sustainable Development Goal 12 (Responsible Consumption and Production). The work demonstrates the value of practical integration across vision, sensing, and decision logic in real agricultural contexts.

title: RLDALS: Reinforcement Learning-Guided Domain-Specific Language Model Framework for Approximate Logic Synthesis
authors: Weichuan Zuo, Jienan Chen, Weiqiang Liu, Weikang Qian
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems
abstract: Approximate computing offers a promising path toward energy- and area-efficient hardware, yet existing design methods often lack generality and global optimization capability. To address these issues, we present RLDALS, a Reinforcement Learning-Guided Domain-Specific Language Model Framework for Approximate Logic Synthesis. By encoding circuit structures as textual sequences, RLDALS reformulates logic approximation as a language-understandable task, enabling semantic knowledge transfer from natural language to electronic design automation. A customized circuit tokenizer embeds structural information into the textual domain, effectively handling dynamic-node variations that challenge graph-based approaches. Reinforcement learning further guides efficient exploration beyond greedy local optima. Moreover, a compensation mechanism is developed to flexibly adapt to diverse error scenarios with negligible hardware overhead, mitigating accuracy loss while preserving hardware gains. Experimental results show that RLDALS achieves an average of 45.9% area reduction with negligible performance loss and simultaneously improves both signal-to-noise ratio and area efficiency on DSP benchmarks, demonstrating strong generality and effectiveness in approximate logic synthesis.

title: Zero-Delay Guard OTFS for OOBE Suppression and Low-Complexity Off-Grid Channel Estimation
authors: Jiasong Han, Xuehan Wang, Jintao Wang, Qingqing Cheng, Yu Zhang
abstract: Orthogonal time frequency space (OTFS) is promising for high-mobility communications, yet practical rectangular-pulse OTFS suffers from severe out-of-band emission (OOBE) and high-complexity off-grid channel estimation. In this letter, we propose a zero-delay guard (ZDG) OTFS frame structure for joint OOBE suppression and pilot-aided channel estimation. By showing that the slot-boundary discontinuity is determined by the zero-delay delay-Doppler (DD) domain column, the proposed scheme nulls this column to enforce boundary continuity and reduce spectral leakage. Meanwhile, the nulled column serves as a built-in guard region, yielding interference-isolated pilot observations without introducing an additional guard column. Based on the resulting observation structure and DD-domain channel sparsity, we further develop a partially-decoupled two-dimensional Newtonized orthogonal matching pursuit (2D-NOMP) algorithm, in which Doppler shifts are estimated first and the delay estimation is subsequently carried out conditioned on the detected Doppler components. Simulation results show that the proposed scheme achieves about 20 dB OOBE reduction relative to conventional OTFS, while attaining competitive channel estimation performance with substantially lower computational complexity.

title: Reinforcement Learning for Control Systems with Time Delays: a Comprehensive Survey
authors: Armando Alves Neto
abstract: In the last decade, Reinforcement Learning (RL) has achieved remarkable success in the control and decision-making of complex dynamical systems. However, most RL algorithms rely on the Markov Decision Process assumption, which is violated in practical cyber-physical systems affected by sensing delays, actuation latencies, and communication constraints. Such time delays introduce memory effects that can significantly degrade performance and compromise stability, particularly in networked and multi-agent environments. This paper presents a comprehensive survey of RL methods designed to address time delays in control systems. We first formalize the main classes of delays and analyze their impact on the Markov property.We then systematically categorize existing approaches into five major families: state augmentation and history-based representations, recurrent policies with learned memory, predictor-based and model-aware methods, robust and domain-randomized training strategies, and safe RL frameworks with explicit constraint handling. For each family, we discuss underlying principles, practical advantages, and inherent limitations. A comparative analysis highlights key trade-offs among these approaches and provides practical guidelines for selecting suitable methods under different delay characteristics and safety requirements. Finally, we identify open challenges and promising research directions, including stability certification, large-delay learning, multi-agent communication co-design, and standardized benchmarking. This survey aims to serve as a unified reference for researchers and practitioners developing reliable RL-based controllers in delay-affected cyber-physical systems.

title: FIT-Print: Towards False-claim-resistant Model Ownership Verification via Targeted Fingerprint
authors: Shuo Shao, Haozhe Zhu, Yiming Li, Hongwei Yao, Tianwei Zhang, Zhan Qin
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Forensics and Security
abstract: Model fingerprinting has emerged as a crucial mechanism for safeguarding the intellectual property of open-source models, offering a non-intrusive approach that requires no modifications to the protected model. However, our analysis reveals that existing fingerprinting techniques are fundamentally vulnerable to false claim attacks, wherein adversaries can fraudulently assert ownership over independent third-party models. We demonstrate that this vulnerability stems from the untargeted nature of current methods, which evaluate model similarity based on arbitrary sample outputs rather than alignment with a specific, predefined reference. To mitigate this vulnerability, we introduce FIT-Print, a targeted fingerprinting paradigm that actively counters false claim attacks. Specifically, FIT-Print leverages optimization to transform the fingerprint into a verifiable, targeted signature. Building upon this foundation, we propose two black-box fingerprinting methods, the bit-wise FIT-ModelDiff and the list-wise FIT-LIME, which utilize output distances and feature attributions as robust model signatures, respectively. Extensive evaluations across benchmark models and datasets show that our framework perfectly neutralizes false claim attacks (100% defense success rate) and eliminates false alarms on independent models (0.0%), all while maintaining a 100% ownership verification rate against diverse model reuse techniques.

title: Traceable, Fair and Privacy-Preserving Decentralized Medical Data Exchange
authors: Rui Song, Yubo Song, Xiaotie Deng, Bin Xiao
abstract: To harness the commercial potential of medical data, various blockchain-based data-sharing and exchange platforms have been proposed. A key challenge is accurately tracing the provenance and transformations of medical data assets throughout the exchange process. Existing methods cannot facilitate exchanges of publicly hosted datasets while maintaining fairness, as they require revealing keys to the blockchain during interactions. This paper presents MEDET, a novel data exchange scheme that ensures traceability of medical data assets while protecting data privacy and guaranteeing exchange fairness. MEDET leverages zero-knowledge proofs to securely verify transformations within medical datasets and confirm data authenticity. Unlike previous schemes, MEDET supports both simple data exchanges and detailed tracking of data transformations and transaction histories, aiding in the provenance and value assessment of medical records. Additionally, MEDET features a key-secure protocol for fair exchange without disclosing symmetric keys. Compared to existing fair exchange schemes, MEDET uniquely ensures the privacy of publicly hosted data while simultaneously upholding the exchange fairness. The security analysis of MEDET demonstrates its security and privacy properties. The evaluation of MEDET indicates that it outperforms existing schemes in tracking data transformations and facilitating exchanges.

title: Multi-Label Image Classification via Contrastive Co-occurrence Learning
authors: Xuelin Zhu, Jian Liu, Dongqi Tang, Weijia Liu, Jiawei Ge, Bo Liu, Jiuxin Cao
abstract: Multi-label image classification is an essential task in computer vision that aims to identify multiple objects in images. Recently, there has been growing research interest in modeling the relationships between labels to enhance label representation learning. An intuitive approach is to train a network to estimate label co-occurrence probabilities in a supervised manner, which are then leveraged to guide the interactions between label representations. However, the extreme sparsity of label co-occurrence signals poses substantial challenges. To address this issue, we commence by examining the potential interaction behaviors between label representations under the guidance of ground-truth label co-occurrence signals. Inspired by our findings, a novel contrastive learning mechanism is crafted to mimic and enhance such behaviors, facilitating effective label representation interactions without relying on explicit supervision from label co-occurrence signals. Based on this, we develop a pioneering contrastive co-occurrence learning framework, which operates on the instance-level label co-occurrence graph for multi-label image classification. This framework involves sequential processes of label representation learning followed by co-occurrence perception learning. Cross-entropy loss for label classification learning and contrastive loss for co-occurrence perception learning are used to jointly optimize the entire framework end-to-end. In this way, label representations can interact effectively, fully perceiving their co-occurrence relationships at the instance level, thereby significantly improving the performance in label recognition. Extensive experiments on public benchmarks demonstrate the superiority of the proposed framework in multi-label image classification. Codes are available on https://github.com/jasonseu/CoCo.

title: Enhancing Frequency Regulation in Modern Power Systems using ANN – Based Virtual Inertia Control
authors: P.I.D.T. Bala Durai Kannan, K. Ravi
abstract: This paper provides an Artificial Neural Network-based Virtual Inertia and Damping Control (ANN-VIDC) technique to enhance frequency stability in power systems with high renewable energy penetration. The suggested controller dynamically modifies virtual inertia and damping settings utilising system indications such as frequency deviation, rate of change of frequency (RoCoF) and variations in renewable power output. The control framework is examined in both islanded and interconnected microgrid setups and contrasted with standard approaches such Fuzzy Logic-based Virtual Inertia Control (FL-VIC) and Model Predictive Control-based VIDC (MPC-VIDC). Simulation findings reveal that the proposed ANN-VIDC provides enhanced frequency regulation, less overshoot and faster stabilization under different operating conditions. The controller successfully mitigates RoCoF and promotes system robustness by adaptively modifying inertia and damping gains in response to system disturbances. These results indicate that the proposed ANN-VIDC technique is a potential and scalable solution for preserving frequency stability in inverter-dominated power systems with significant levels of renewable energy integration.

title: Enhancing VFD Tolerance Under Voltage Sag Based on Improved Control Strategies and Two-Stage Solved Method of Control Parameters
authors: Shuyuan Zhang, Ying Wang, Yushan Yin, Qiming Zhong, Jingyi Li
abstract: Variable frequency drives (VFDs) are sensitive to voltage sags, because the typical VFD control systems are designed for steady operation, the ability of ride-through the voltage sag is not the main concern of the design. However, sophisticated manufacturing has extremely stringent requirements for rotational speed, and rotational speed drops caused by voltage sag will result in industrial disruption. Enhancing VFD tolerance under voltage sag has become essential to modern industrial development. Therefore, this study proposes improved control strategies and two-stage solved method for control parameters to enhance VFD tolerance under voltage sag. First, the response characteristics of induction motors IM under voltage sag are analyzed, which supports the idea of improving control strategies and solving control parameters. Second, this study proposes an improved control strategy, including the voltage feedforward control to improve timeliness, the variable stage control to achieve energy transfer and improve controllability by dynamically adjusting rotor flux, and the electromagnetic torque feedback control to improve accuracy. Third, the two-stage solved method of control parameters is proposed. Stage I constructs the solution spaces by control window least squares, and stage II solves the optimal control parameter values by improved gray wolf optimization, enabling adaptive adjustment of control parameters in each control window. Finally, experimental results verify that the proposed method can tolerate voltage sags with a residual voltage of 0.63 p.u. and can tolerate 0.62 p.u. for 132 ms, verifying its effectiveness.

title: Dimension-reduced Koopman based Model Predictive Control for Discrete-time Nonlinear Systems Via Block Coordinate Descent
authors: Hanqiu Bao, Yangzhen Liu, Qi Kang, Feng Yang, Lingfei Xiao, Feng Qian, Wang Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: This work proposes a Dimension-Reduced Koopman Model Predictive Control (Dr-KMPC) framework for discrete-time nonlinear systems. The main challenges in existing Koopman-based control approaches arise from the curse of dimensionality associated with dictionary-based lifting and the computational burden of deep learning-based alternatives. Particularly, standard Extended Dynamic Mode Decomposition (EDMD) often requires a high-dimensional lifted space to ensure accuracy, which renders the subsequent controller design computationally expensive. To address these issues, we introduce a trainable linear encoding map to project high-dimensional observables into a lower-dimensional latent space. The resulting non-convex joint parameter learning problem is solved by using a novel Block Coordinate Descent (BCD) algorithm, which decomposes the optimization into sequentially solvable, strictly convex linear least-squares sub-problems. The resulting Dr- KMPC formulation allows standard Quadratic Programming (QP) solvers to determine optimal control inputs efficiently. Rigorous theoretical analysis establishes recursive feasibility and asymptotic stability of the proposed MPC method. Simulation results show that the proposed method can effectively handle nonlinear systems and achieve high control performance.

title: Mutual Coupling-Aware Channel Estimation for Holographic MIMO Systems: A Vector Factorization Design Paradigm
authors: Anzheng Tang, Shenghui Song, Chi-Ying Tsui, Rodrigo C. de Lamare, Merouane Debbah
abstract: Holographic multiple-input and multiple-output (HMIMO) systems have emerged as a promising technology for enabling precise wavefront control and achieving fine-grained spatial resolution thanks to their capability to manipulate electromagnetic fields. However, the dense antenna configurations may lead to severe mutual coupling (MC) effects, which significantly complicates channel state information acquisition. To address this challenge, this paper investigates channel estimation for HMIMO systems with explicit consideration of MC effects. Specifically, we first derive a MC model based on multiport theory. It is shown that the Toeplitz structure of MC matrix utilized in conventional MIMO systems does not persist in HMIMO systems. As a result, existing channel estimation algorithms cannot be directly applied to HMIMO systems. To bridge this gap, we propose an approximate unitary diagonalization representation for the MC matrix via plane decomposition, which significantly reduces the number of parameters to be estimated. Moreover, by leveraging the wavenumber-domain sparsity of the HMIMO channel, we formulate the MC-aware channel estimation as a vector factorization (VF) problem within a variational Bayesian inference framework, where Markov chain and pattern-coupling priors are incorporated to capture the statistical characteristics of both the wavenumber-domain channel and the MC coefficients. To effectively resolve the dependencies induced by the structured priors, we further propose a bilinear VF-tailored hybrid message passing (BiVF-HMP) algorithm, which exploits the power of variational inference, belief propagation, and the first-order optimality. Simulation results validate the effectiveness of the proposed BiVF-HMP algorithm and highlight the importance of considering MC effects for channel estimation.

title: A Multiterminal Solid-State Transformer for MV/LV Power Routing in Hybrid AC/DC Smart Grids
authors: Sérgio Coelho, João L. Afonso, Vitor Monteiro
abstract: Conventional power grid infrastructures, marked by rigidity and technological obsolescence, struggle to meet the demands of modern power systems. Furthermore, low-frequency transformers (LFT) lack controllability and multifunctional capability, while their operational lifetime reduces under the widespread use of nonlinear loads. Thus, achieving stable operation requires advanced power electronics converters and a shift towards hybrid AC/DC smart grids, both at the medium-voltage (MV) distribution level and within low-voltage (LV) microgrids. To address these goals, this article proposes a multiterminal solid-state transformer (SST) for MV/LV power routing. As a distinguishing feature, it integrates an isolated DC/DC quad-active bridge (QAB) converter as the intermediate power stage, unifying and providing complete galvanic isolation among four electrical ports: 1) MVAC; 2) MVDC; 3) LVAC; and 4) LVDC. A downscaled SiC-based prototype, switching at 50 kHz, experimentally validates the concept. Explicitly, tests on the QAB stage under distinct power flow scenarios confirm the feasibility of the proposed SST, establishing it as a promising solution in hybrid AC/DC smart grids.

title: A Low-Voltage Driven Thermoacoustic Loudspeaker Based on PBFDO Film
authors: Ye Zheng, Siwei Sun, Zitong Zhou, Debo Wang
abstract: Constrained by the limited electrical conductivity of traditional nanomaterials, existing thermoacoustic (TA) loudspeakers typically rely on high driving voltages, which severely restricts their application in flexible wearable devices. To address this challenge, an ultra-highly conductive n-type polymer, poly(benzodifurandione) (PBFDO), was introduced for the first time to fabricate a highly efficient, low-voltage-driven TA loudspeaker, and a theoretical model for its electro-thermo-acoustic conversion was established. The acoustic test results demonstrate outstanding comprehensive performance, as the device achieves a sound pressure level of 68.9 dB and a voltage-normalized efficiency of 5.4 × 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-2</sup> Pa/V at 1 V<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">AC</sub>, while sustaining an effective 36.88 dB output even under an extreme 0.013 V<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">AC</sub> input. Furthermore, the device exhibits an extremely low hysteresis error (2.34%), highly stable continuous operation (attenuation of only 0.261 dB), outstanding bending flexibility (fluctuation < 0.022 dB), and cyclic fatigue resistance (fluctuation < 0.518 dB), alongside reliable on-skin thermal safety. This work successfully lowers the driving voltage to the millivolt level, paving a new material and design pathway for next-generation flexible wearable audio devices.

title: A DRL-based Joint Optimization Method for Service Placement and Task Offloading in Edge Computing
authors: Bo Su, Rencong Lin, Zhenxin Li, Jie Wang, Hongyi Miao, Aobo Gao, Yewen Cao
abstract: Edge computing has gradually become a critical means of addressing the latency and bandwidth bottlenecks inherent in cloud computing. By deploying computing and storage resources at network edge nodes, edge computing is capable of providing low-latency and high-bandwidth services to Internet of Things (IoT) devices. To handle heterogeneous computing tasks at edge nodes, the corresponding services should be pre-placed in advance, including image installation and data caching. In this study, we focus on the edge computing system that considers service placement. Taking into full account the latency and energy consumption incurred during task offloading and execution, as well as the additional cost introduced by service deployment, the joint optimization of service placement and task offloading decisions becomes exceedingly complex. Based on deep reinforcement learning, we propose a novel method that integrates Double DQN, dueling networks, and prioritized experience replay to make service placement and task offloading decisions, aiming to minimize the long-term weighted sum of task latency and energy consumption. Extensive simulation experiments demonstrate that our proposed method achieves stable convergence and outperforms other baseline methods.

title: VC-VTON: Towards Across-View and Multi-Posture Driven Virtual Try-On via Spatiotemporal-Aware View Consistency Training
authors: Zhiyuan Ma, Jiabao Wei, Zhihan Cai, Chundi Yang, Ermo Hua, Shulei Xie, Jianjun Li, Bowen Zhou
abstract: Virtual try-on (VTON) aims to synthesize specific fashion images dressed in given garments, which possesses great potential in real-world scenarios. Existing methods generally stand on the shoulder of the single-view VTON to train a warping model and then fit the given garments onto the human body under a fixed posture and viewpoint, which often fails to preserve the consistent garment characteristics in across-view and multi-pose guided try-on scenarios due to the lack of both across-view data and effective view consistency training. To alleviate this dilemma, we propose a fresh view consistency-driven VTON task (VC-VTON) and release a multi-view virtual try-on dataset with complete annotation (e.g., viewpoint, text, posture, parsing maps, etc.) to encourage across-view training scenarios. Based on this hard-won dataset, we further propose VC-TwinNet, a Twin-UNet baseline based on spatiotemporal-aware View Consistency training, designed specifically for the challenging task. Specifically, to enable view-aware denoising and sparse-to-continuous view generalization, we introduce RoPE and circle embedding to represent the relative and continuous position relation across viewpoints, serving to distinguish their outfitting appearance and warping states. Afterwards, to implicitly learn the interactions across views under given multiple posture conditions, we further contribute a spatiotemporal-aware view attention module to capture the spatial and temporal details for across-view training. Moreover, we utilize an across-view consistency loss to supervise the model training, to ultimately improve the performance of our VC-VTON. Extensive experiments demonstrate the superiority of our approach and state-of-the-art results on various evaluations without declining single-view performance.<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup> And as for practicality and timeliness, our proposed components are essentially plug-and-play and remain effective in the new DiT-centered paradigm.

title: A Clustering-Based Framework for Identifying Suspicious Trading Patterns in Capital Market
authors: Asif Zaman, Romona Magdalene Sarkar, Sabiha Khair Ohi, Iftekharul Mobin
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence &amp; Networking (QPAIN)
abstract: Market manipulation is the dubious practice of manipulating stock prices in order to make a quick profit, which truly degrades confidence on trading platforms. We implemented an unsupervised fraud-detection toolkit that begins with K-Means++ clustering to address this issue. A dataset of roughly one million financial transactions from 2012 to 2024 is used. In order to identify fraudulent trades and categorize them using market practice heuristic thresholds, the study suggests a clustering-based pipeline. The method highlights 2.02% of trades as suspicious where 51.10% clearly indicate spoofing, 0.10% indicate pump and dump, 0.55% indicate insider trading, 1.43% indicate a fake breakout, and 46.83% are unclassified. Despite the lack of ground truth, the model's performance is confirmed by a Silhouette Score of 0.561.
